title: Learning German Independently
excerpt:
You can, of course, also learn German independently – without attending a course or in addition to a course. Your »»Helper Circle can support your learning. One great place to learn German independently is in a library:
Nuremberg City Library has many language courses with audio CDs, language-learning software, dictionaries and other materials for learning German.
You have to pay an annual fee to borrow things from the library. Adults aged 18 and over pay €20 for one year. Students, school pupils and Nuremberg Pass holders pay €10 for one year. For children and teenagers under the age of 18, borrowing things is free of charge.
Learning in the library and using the WiFi and the internet-connected PCs are free of charge.
You can take items home with you – books can be kept for 28 days and DVDs for 14 days.After four weeks, you will have to return everything.  Important: if you don't take the items back in time, you will have to pay high fines!
This short film talks about libraries as places of learning:»» Film.
In addition to the Central City Library, there are six district libraries in Nuremberg. The six district libraries have also had free Wi-Fi since 2017. Addresses, opening hours and more information can be found for all district libraries here:»»Central City Library and the District Libraries.
There are also lots of ways of learning German for free on the internet.
The German Volkshochschulverband (DVV) runs the free website IWDL – which stands for »»I Want to Learn German. This online service offers German courses from A1 level up to B1 and uses the integration course learning plan alongside digital learning materials. The website is not only useful for independent learners – the materials and supporting media are also helpful if you're on an integration course or another kind of German course.
The»»Goethe Institute has a website which is free to use and has lots of practice exercises for learning the German you'll need in the world of work. The exercises are specially adapted to certain job categories.
The radio station »»Deutsche Welle offers online language exercises that use video clips and video series. They are matched to the language levels A1 to C and are a fun addition to any other German course you might be studying.
On the »»Tandempartners.org website, you can look for and find language exchange partners. Just enter your first language, the language you want to learn and the name of the place you live in. You can also meet nice people along the way.
 Ankommen (Arrival) App:  In addition to information on life in Germany and on asylum, vocational training and work, the “Ankommen” app also includes a self-study course (starting at level A1) for your first few weeks in Germany.
 Serlo ABC: A free app for learning to read, either as self-study or as a supplement to a language course. This app has been approved for use in integration courses.
 Mein Deutschbuch (My German book): An extensive resource for learning the German language. This website is designed for learners and teachers, levels A1-B2.
 Deutsch-Uni Online (DUO): Language-learning programme for A1 to C2.
 MeinVokabular (My Vocabulary): This app from HWK lets you create your own dictionary and vocabulary flash cards using photos.
 AsylPlus Deutsch lernen (Learning German): An overview of various German-language learning options.
 „Die Stadt der Wörter“ (“City of Words”) An app and website for fun vocabulary learning for those without previous knowledge – in German, English, French and other languages.
 VHS-Lernportal" (VHS Learning Portal): Free German language courses. You can also learn to read, write and do arithmetic. There are also resources to help you prepare for the school-leaving exams in German, maths and English.
 Einstufungstest Sprache (Language Assessment Test): onSET-online language assessment test for refugees and for officials and volunteers to assess language ability

This might also interest you: »»Learning Spots
content:
You can, of course, also learn German independently – without attending a course or in addition to a course. Your »»Helper Circle can support your learning. One great place to learn German independently is in a library:

Nuremberg City Library has many language courses with audio CDs, language-learning software, dictionaries and other materials for learning German.

You have to pay an annual fee to borrow things from the library. Adults aged 18 and over pay €20 for one year. Students, school pupils and Nuremberg Pass holders pay €10 for one year. For children and teenagers under the age of 18, borrowing things is free of charge.

Learning in the library and using the WiFi and the internet-connected PCs are free of charge.

You can take items home with you – books can be kept for 28 days and DVDs for 14 days.After four weeks, you will have to return everything.
Important: if you don't take the items back in time, you will have to pay high fines!

This short film talks about libraries as places of learning:»» Film.

In addition to the Central City Library, there are six district libraries in Nuremberg. The six district libraries have also had free Wi-Fi since 2017. Addresses, opening hours and more information can be found for all district libraries here:»»Central City Library and the District Libraries.

There are also lots of ways of learning German for free on the internet.

The German Volkshochschulverband (DVV) runs the free website IWDL – which stands for »»I Want to Learn German. This online service offers German courses from A1 level up to B1 and uses the integration course learning plan alongside digital learning materials. The website is not only useful for independent learners – the materials and supporting media are also helpful if you're on an integration course or another kind of German course.

The»»Goethe Institute has a website which is free to use and has lots of practice exercises for learning the German you'll need in the world of work. The exercises are specially adapted to certain job categories.

The radio station »»Deutsche Welle offers online language exercises that use video clips and video series. They are matched to the language levels A1 to C and are a fun addition to any other German course you might be studying.

On the »»Tandempartners.org website, you can look for and find language exchange partners. Just enter your first language, the language you want to learn and the name of the place you live in. You can also meet nice people along the way.

Ankommen (Arrival) App:  In addition to information on life in Germany and on asylum, vocational training and work, the “Ankommen” app also includes a self-study course (starting at level A1) for your first few weeks in Germany.

Serlo ABC: A free app for learning to read, either as self-study or as a supplement to a language course. This app has been approved for use in integration courses.

Mein Deutschbuch (My German book): An extensive resource for learning the German language. This website is designed for learners and teachers, levels A1-B2.

Deutsch-Uni Online (DUO): Language-learning programme for A1 to C2.

MeinVokabular (My Vocabulary): This app from HWK lets you create your own dictionary and vocabulary flash cards using photos.

AsylPlus Deutsch lernen (Learning German): An overview of various German-language learning options.

„Die Stadt der Wörter“ (“City of Words”) An app and website for fun vocabulary learning for those without previous knowledge – in German, English, French and other languages.

VHS-Lernportal" (VHS Learning Portal): Free German language courses. You can also learn to read, write and do arithmetic. There are also resources to help you prepare for the school-leaving exams in German, maths and English.

Einstufungstest Sprache (Language Assessment Test): onSET-online language assessment test for refugees and for officials and volunteers to assess language ability

This might also interest you:
»»Learning Spots

title: How do I find an interpreter?
excerpt:
You don't speak German very well yet? Do you have to go to an authority? Then an interpreter can help you. It also helps when you visit the doctor. Or at parents' evenings at school or kindergarten. You will then understand better what people want to say to you. 
Some authorities and organisations have their own interpreters. The authorities can use them in their counselling sessions. Make an appointment for this. Is the interpreter free of charge? Always ask about this.



You can call the Centre for Active Citizens (ZAB) for discussions in schools or daycare centres. They will provide you with a volunteer interpreter. The interpreter will go with you to the appointment. Interpreters work confidentially. They will not tell you anything else. They do not give specialist advice. They do not give legal advice.
You can get help in the following languages: Albanian, Arabic, Bosnian, Bulgarian, English, French, Greek, Italian, Croatian, Polish, Portuguese, Romanian, Russian, Serbian, Spanish, Turkish, Hungarian, Vietnamese and Belarusian. 


Contact
                    
                        Hassan Khalaf
                    
                    
                        |
                    
                    
                        Zentrum Aktiver Bürger (ZAB) | Ansprechpartner
                    
                Gostenhofer Hauptstraße 63, 90443 Nürnbergkhalaf@iska-nuernberg.de+49 91192971727+49 15737560391https://www.iska-nuernberg.de/zab/allesrundumskind.html

 Do you need an official translation? Official documents are: Certificates, marriage certificates or birth certificates. They may only be translated by state-certified or sworn translators. These translators are recognised by the court. The translation is then notarised. It is accepted by the authorities. These translations often cost a lot of money.
Before you translate: Check first:

Do you really need a certified translation?
Then compare the prices from different providers.

You can search for state-certified interpreters in the database:
 www.justiz-dolmetscher.de

title: Education, Jobs and University
excerpt:
To find a good job in Germany, it is important to have done a vocational training course or academic studies. On the following pages, you will find information on training options, getting started with work and studying at university, and contact details for people who can help you along the way.
When you need advice on issues related to education, you can find it in the »»Database of Education Services for Newly Arrived Migrants, which provides educational options for new Nurembergers and a lot of additional supporting information. The database can help your advisor to find the right option for you.
content:
To find a good job in Germany, it is important to have done a vocational training course or academic studies. On the following pages, you will find information on training options, getting started with work and studying at university, and contact details for people who can help you along the way.

When you need advice on issues related to education, you can find it in the »»Database of Education Services for Newly Arrived Migrants, which provides educational options for new Nurembergers and a lot of additional supporting information. The database can help your advisor to find the right option for you.

title: Access to the Job Market
excerpt:
Rules on access to the labour market
People from the EU are allowed to work in Germany. They do not need any special permission to do so. Different rules apply to refugees. Whether they are allowed to work depends on their residence status. The rules are explained below.Further down, you will also find advice centres. There you can get help and information.
Asylum seekers with proof of arrival or a temporary residence permit:
As long as you are required to live in an initial reception centre, you are not allowed to work. After that, the »»Immigration Authority may grant you permission to work. To do so, you must submit an application.
Several factors are important for obtaining a work permit:

Three months after registering as an asylum seeker in Germany (with proof of arrival or a temporary residence permit), you have ‘subordinate’ access to the labour market. This means that the Federal Employment Agency checks whether there is a priority worker available for the position. The working conditions are also assessed.
After 15 months, the priority check is no longer carried out. However, the working conditions are still assessed.
After 48 months, you have full access to the labour market. There are no longer any restrictions. However, you must apply for this at the Foreigners’ Registration Office and it must be stated in your temporary residence permit.
If you wish to undertake in-company training, the decision rests solely with the Foreigners’ Registration Office. There is no priority check. Nor are the working conditions assessed by the Federal Employment Agency.

Persons with ‘tolerated status’:

If you have a ‘Duldung’ (tolerated status), the Foreigners’ Registration Office always decides on your work permit. The Foreigners’ Registration Office may impose a general ban on working under immigration law.
If no such work ban applies to you, as a person with ‘Duldung’ status you have subordinate access to the labour market from the first day of your ‘Duldung’.
Otherwise, the same labour market requirements as set out above apply.

Refugees with a residence permit:

If you have been recognised by the Federal Office for Migration and Refugees (BAMF) as a person entitled to asylum, a refugee or a person entitled to subsidiary protection, the Foreigners’ Registration Office will issue you with a residence permit. This entitles you to take up any gainful employment and thus grants you full access to the labour market.
Different rules apply to self-employment!
A specific job offer is ALWAYS a prerequisite for the above-mentioned applications

Rules on access to the labour market

People from the EU are allowed to work in Germany. They do not need any special permission to do so. 
Different rules apply to refugees. Whether they are allowed to work depends on their residence status. The rules are explained below.
Further down, you will also find advice centres. There you can get help and information.

Asylum seekers with proof of arrival or a temporary residence permit:

As long as you are required to live in an initial reception centre, you are not allowed to work. After that, the »»Immigration Authority may grant you permission to work. To do so, you must submit an application.

Several factors are important for obtaining a work permit:

  • Three months after registering as an asylum seeker in Germany (with proof of arrival or a temporary residence permit), you have ‘subordinate’ access to the labour market. This means that the Federal Employment Agency checks whether there is a priority worker available for the position. The working conditions are also assessed.
  • After 15 months, the priority check is no longer carried out. However, the working conditions are still assessed.
  • After 48 months, you have full access to the labour market. There are no longer any restrictions. However, you must apply for this at the Foreigners’ Registration Office and it must be stated in your temporary residence permit.
  • If you wish to undertake in-company training, the decision rests solely with the Foreigners’ Registration Office. There is no priority check. Nor are the working conditions assessed by the Federal Employment Agency.

Persons with ‘tolerated status’:

  • If you have a ‘Duldung’ (tolerated status), the Foreigners’ Registration Office always decides on your work permit. The Foreigners’ Registration Office may impose a general ban on working under immigration law.
  • If no such work ban applies to you, as a person with ‘Duldung’ status you have subordinate access to the labour market from the first day of your ‘Duldung’.
  • Otherwise, the same labour market requirements as set out above apply.

Refugees with a residence permit:

  • If you have been recognised by the Federal Office for Migration and Refugees (BAMF) as a person entitled to asylum, a refugee or a person entitled to subsidiary protection, the Foreigners’ Registration Office will issue you with a residence permit. This entitles you to take up any gainful employment and thus grants you full access to the labour market.
  • Different rules apply to self-employment!
  • A specific job offer is ALWAYS a prerequisite for the above-mentioned applications

title: Recognition of foreign educational qualifications
excerpt:
In Germany, certificates and diplomas are very important for school, university or work. They show what you have already learnt and achieved. If you have certificates, you can have them recognised in Germany. The Recognition Counselling Service can help you with this. The counselling is free of charge. They will tell you where to go, what documents you need and what it costs. The advice centre will support you with the recognition procedure. It will inform you who you need to contact and tell you where you can get financial help. The aim is for you to find a profession that matches your qualifications. 
Specialist advice centre for recognition:
City of Nuremberg, Bildungszentrum im Bildungscampus - Central Service Centre for the Recognition of Foreign Qualifications in the Nuremberg Metropolitan Region
Central IQ Advice Centre for the Recognition of Foreign Qualifications (ZAQ+) Schoppershofstraße 80, 90489 Nuremberg  or  Theresienstraße 18 (1st floor), 90403 Nuremberg
 0911 231-10552  Recognition of foreign qualifications - ZAQ+ | Education Centre Nuremberg
If you are planning to study at a college/university, please contact the ""contact persons at the universities to have your certificates recognised.
content:
In Germany, certificates and diplomas are very important for school, university or work. They show what you have already learnt and achieved. If you have certificates, you can have them recognised in Germany.

The Recognition Counselling Service can help you with this. The counselling is free of charge. They will tell you where to go, what documents you need and what it costs.

The advice centre will support you with the recognition procedure. It will inform you who you need to contact and tell you where you can get financial help. The aim is for you to find a profession that matches your qualifications.

title: School (6 to 15 years old)
excerpt:
In Germany, there is something called "Schulpflicht" - this means "school obligation". This means that in Germany, all children aged between 6 and 15 are legally required to go to school. The parents or guardians have a duty to ensure that their children attend school regularly. Going to state and municipal schools is free of cost.
The school often gives children written information to take home with them and give to the parents. It is important that you read this information. If find it hard to understand these letters, ask your »»Asylum Advice Centre or your»»Helper Circle for someone who can help you.

School Registration
The supervisors of your accommodation will register your child with the State Education Office. You will get a letter inviting you to an appointment at the Education Authority. After that, your child can go to school.
Important: Be sure to bring your child with you! Please also bring an interpreter and the following documents:

Your ID, your birth certificate or your arrival certificate (Ankunftsnachweis)
Registration certificate from the Immigration Office (Ausländerbehörde)
All documents concerning your child (ID, birth certificate, certificates, medical certificates, etc.)

State Education Office in the City of Nuremberg (Staatliches Schulamt in der Stadt Nürnberg)   Lina-Ammon-Str. 28 , 90471 Nürnberg Registration Monday - Friday 08:00 - 13:00appointments can also be arranged by telephone


The Bavarian School System
If required, your child will usually be placed in a transitional class to learn German. If your child can speak German well enough, they will be placed in a normal school class. Sometimes, however, there are not enough places in the transitional classes, so it might be the case that your child is placed straight into a regular class.
Class trips, excursions, school trips, school performances and swimming lessons are part of the lessons and the school day in Germany. Your child should participate in these classes, not just because they can learn a lot of new things, but also because these joint activities improve children’s enjoyment of school and strengthen their linguistic abilities.
Here you can find information about the Bavarian school system in»»German.
Do you have any questions about the Bavarian school system?
NEST parent guides helps children and parents to find their bearings in the Bavarian school system.
Nuremberg Parents' Office for School Success and Participation (NEST)  Magdalena Musial  +49 (0) 911 231-14204  Magdalena.Musial@stadt.nuernberg.de  Wednesday 09:00 - 12:00 and 16:00 - 18:00 Thursday 09:00 - 12:00 or by appointment. During school holidays, the advice centre is closed. Advice provided in German and in the families' languages www.nuernberg.de/internet/paedagogisches_institut/


Special Programmes for Refugee Children and Teenagers
There are also special programmes which enable refugee children and teenagers to get started at a secondary school or a "Gymnasium" (an academic high school which focuses on preparing pupils for university):
Entry into high school: The SPRINT programme (intensive language training) is aimed at children and young people who are in transitional classes and have less knowledge of German. After attending a transitional class, the Education Authority can choose school children that it thinks are ready for high school (secondary school). Integration into year group 6 or 7 opens the way for pupils to gain their secondary school diplomas.
SPRINT (Intensive Language Training) sprint@mb-rs-mittelfranken.de
A Different Path to the Gymnasium (Academic High School): For particularly talented and motivated school pupils aged up to 15, InGym offers a six-month German course which is intended to pave the way to the Gymnasium. Registration takes place via a Gymnasium that is located nearby. The child will initially attend as a guest student and then move on to the Martin-Behaim-Gymnasium.
InGym mbg@martin-behaim-gymnasium.de


School Supplies
You will receive confirmation from the school that your child is registered for the school. Upon receiving confirmation, you can apply to the Social Welfare Office for funding for school supplies. This amounts in total to €100, which is paid out either in one or two instalments. This depends on when your child starts school.
Sozialamt der Stadt Nürnberg (Social Welfare Office of the City of Nuremberg) Financial Support (Wirtschaftliche Hilfen)  Frauentorgraben 17, 90443 Nürnberg  +49 (0) 911 231-5513  Monday - Friday 08:30 - 12:30Please make an appointment


Travel Costs to School
You can send an application to the Education Authority for your child's bus and train costs to be subsidised. This means that you don't have to pay your child's travel costs youself. This is only possible if your child's school route is longer than 2km (1st to 4th classes). From the 5th class onwards, the route to school must be longer than 3km. For further information and to download application forms, please click here:
 www.schulen-in-nuernberg.de


Telling the School that Your Child Is Ill
Please call the school before 08:00 if your child is ill or if there is another reason that your child can't go to school. Otherwise, the school is obliged to look for your child, if necessary with the police. You may have to pay a fine.


Further Support for Pupils in Nuremberg
It is possible to receive financial support for your child with the »»Education and Participation Package, which covers school lunches, membership fees for cultural or sports clubs or money for participating in school trips. You must submit the application to the Social Welfare Office without delay.
Important: You must always submit the application before you pay for something yourself. If you have already paid for something, you will not get the money back.
If you live in the postcode area 90402, 90403, 90408, 90409, 90411, 90419, 90425, 90427, 90429, 90431, 90439, 90443, 90449, 90482, 90489 or 90491, please contact:
Social Welfare Office of the City of Nuremberg (Sozialamt der Stadt Nürnberg)Dienstleistungszentrum Bildung und Teilhabe  Frauentorgraben 17, 90443 Nürnberg Monday to Friday 08:30 - 12:30 for information call +49 (0) 911 231-4347
If you live in postcode area90441, 90451, 90453, 90455, 90459, 90461, 90469, 90471, 90473, 90475, 90478 or 90480, please contact:
Social Welfare Office of the City of Nuremberg (Sozialamt der Stadt Nürnberg)Dienstleistungszentrum Bildung und Teilhabe  Reinerzer Str. 12, 90473 Nürnberg Monday to Friday 08:30 - 12:30 for information call +49 (0) 911 231-4347


Afternoon Care Services
For school children, there is childcare and lunchtime supervision for after-school care. Daycare placements are offered by the Youth Welfare Office of the City of Nuremberg. For lunchtime supervision, please contact your child’s school.
Youth Welfare Office of the City of Nuremberg (Jugendamt der Stadt Nürnberg)Servicestelle Kitaplatz  Dietzstr. 4, Erdgeschoss, Zimmer 002, 90443 Nürnberg  +49 (0) 911 231-10444

 www.schulen-in-nuernberg.de

Telling the School that Your Child Is Ill

Please call the school before 08:00 if your child is ill or if there is another reason that your child can't go to school. Otherwise, the school is obliged to look for your child, if necessary with the police. You may have to pay a fine.

Further Support for Pupils in Nuremberg

It is possible to receive financial support for your child with the »»Education and Participation Package, which covers school lunches, membership fees for cultural or sports clubs or money for participating in school trips. You must submit the application to the Social Welfare Office without delay.

Important: You must always submit the application before you pay for something yourself. If you have already paid for something, you will not get the money back.

If you live in the postcode area
90402, 90403, 90408, 90409, 90411, 90419, 90425, 90427, 90429, 90431, 90439, 90443, 90449, 90482, 90489 or 90491, please contact:

Dienstleistungszentrum Bildung und Teilhabe

 Monday to Friday 08:30 - 12:30
for information call +49 (0) 911 231-4347

If you live in postcode area
90441, 90451, 90453, 90455, 90459, 90461, 90469, 90471, 90473, 90475, 90478 or 90480, please contact:

Dienstleistungszentrum Bildung und Teilhabe
 Reinerzer Str. 12, 90473 Nürnberg
 Monday to Friday 08:30 - 12:30
for information call +49 (0) 911 231-4347

Afternoon Care Services

For school children, there is childcare and lunchtime supervision for after-school care. Daycare placements are offered by the Youth Welfare Office of the City of Nuremberg. For lunchtime supervision, please contact your child’s school.

title: Vocational school (16 - 21 years old)
excerpt:
If you are between 16 and 21 years old, you have to go to vocational school (Berufsschulpflicht). This applies to refugees and newcomers from EU countries as well as Germans.
At vocational school, you will first learn German in professional integration classes and get further education and support in professional orientation.
If you are undertaking a »»dual education, you should also attend vocational school.
Please register at vocational school in person and a maximum of 2 weeks after arriving in Nuremberg. Bring all documents for your previous school career (for example, certificates) and your ID documents (proof of arrival/permission).
Registration, Berufliche Schule V  Building F, Room F 235, Deumentenstraße 1, 90489 Nuremberg
Until you reach 18 years of age, you can get financial support for certain activities at school and in your spare time through the»»educational and participation package.
content:
If you are between 16 and 21 years old, you have to go to vocational school (Berufsschulpflicht). This applies to refugees and newcomers from EU countries as well as Germans.

At vocational school, you will first learn German in professional integration classes and get further education and support in professional orientation.

If you are undertaking a »»dual education, you should also attend vocational school.

Please register at vocational school in person and a maximum of 2 weeks after arriving in Nuremberg. Bring all documents for your previous school career (for example, certificates) and your ID documents (proof of arrival/permission).

Registration, Berufliche Schule V
 Building F, Room F 235, Deumentenstraße 1, 90489 Nuremberg

Until you reach 18 years of age, you can get financial support for certain activities at school and in your spare time through the»»educational and participation package.

title: Education (dual training and full-time school)
excerpt:
In Germany, it is a great advantage to have a professional qualification before starting work. People with professional qualifications earn more money on average over the course of their lives, are less likely to be unemployed, and are less likely to have temporary contracts than those without professional qualifications.
Dual education
The dual training scheme is unique to Germany. Dual training takes place about one third of the time in the »»vocational school and about two thirds of the time in a training company. You can simultaneously learn the theory and practice while earning money at a company during your apprenticeship. In this video, dual training is explained in detail: »»Dual training. Here is the link to the video in Arabic: »»Dual training - Arabic.
Depending on the type of profession you would like to learn in dual training, you should turn to the right place:
Chamber of Commerce (HWK)
The Chamber of Commerce (HWK) is responsible for manual professions. This involves professions such as bakers, bricklayers or painters. The Chamber of Handicrafts has a special contact person for refugees who want to start training in this field. She supports the search for training courses or an initial qualification, which can precede training.
Chamber of Commerce (HWK) Andrea Sitzmann  +49 (0) 911 5309-189  andrea.sitzmann@hwk-mittelfranken.de
Chamber of Industry and Commerce (IHK)
The Chamber of Industry and Commerce (IHK) is responsible for professions in industrial manufacturing, trade and services. This is the case, for example, as a plant mechanic or a trader in wholesale and foreign trade. The Chamber of Industry and Commerce of Nuremberg also has special contact persons to offer advice on training, initial qualification and practical training:
Chamber of Industry and Commerce (IHK) Khaled Khouja, M.A.   +49 (0) 911 1335-225  khaled.khouja@nuernberg.ihk.de, integrationsberatung@nuernberg.ihk.de
The Chamber of Commerce also has an  open office for refugees who are seeking training in industry, trade or service. They are available every Tuesday between 14:00 - 15:00. Before coming to a meeting, please fill out the form, which you can download: »»Form.
Preschool education at the vocational school
In Germany, however, there is not only dual training, but also full-time school education at a vocational school. Vocational training provides a completed vocational training in theory and practice outside of the dual training system (without an apprenticeship at a company). It usually takes two to three and a half years. The course takes place full-time and includes general subjects in addition to the professional subjects. Since the vocational schools differ greatly in terms of their faculties, admission requirements, training duration and further education possibilities, it is advisable to make enquiries at the school in question. In this flyer, you will find information on the versatile urban vocational schools: »»Flyer-BerufsfachschulenA list of all Nuremberg vocational schools - including private schools - can be found here: »»Nuremberg vocational schools.

title: Education and participation package (BuT)
excerpt:
If you receive social benefits, you can apply for support for your child from the "education and participation package".
 This applies:
For children up to their 18th birthday.For young adults up to their 25th birthday if they go to school and do not receive a training allowance.
The following benefits are available:

15 euros per month: Children and young people up to the age of 18 can receive vouchers for courses, leisure activities or sports.
Excursions and school trips: Costs for excursions and trips lasting several days (e.g. travel, meals, admission) are covered.
Lunch: Vouchers are available for shared lunches at school or daycare centres. Snacks from the school kiosk are not paid for.
School supplies: An amount is paid in two instalments per school year for materials such as exercise books, books or photocopying costs.
Learning support: If the school confirms it, vouchers for learning support can be provided.
School transport: In special cases, the costs of travelling to school are covered. Please contact the social welfare office. 

You submit the application to the social welfare office:
If your accommodation has the postcode:90402, 90403, 90408, 90409, 90411, 90419, 90425, 90427, 90429, 90431, 90439, 90443, 90449, 90482, 90489 or 90491, please contact:
Social Welfare Office of the City of Nuremberg Service Centre for Education and Participation Frauentorgraben 17, 90443 Nuremberg
If your accommodation has the postcode90441, 90451, 90453, 90455, 90459, 90461, 90469, 90471, 90473, 90475, 90478 or 90480, please contact:
Social Welfare Office of the City of Nuremberg Service Centre for Education and Participation Reinerzer Str. 12, 90473 Nuremberg
 Monday - Friday 08:30 - 12:30 0911 231-4347
Further information on the education and participation package in Nuremberg can also be found in several foreign languages at
 www.bildung-und-teilhabe.nuernberg.de
Important: When you submit your application, you will receive the vouchers for the period of the social benefit notification, this can also be done retrospectively for a maximum of 1 year.

  • School supplies: An amount is paid in two instalments per school year for materials such as exercise books, books or photocopying costs.

title: Zentrale Anlaufstelle Migration (ZAM) – Beratung
excerpt:
Central Contact Point Migration (ZAM) - Counselling
The "Zentrale Anlaufstelle Migration - Beratung" (ZAM-Be) helps people who have come to Germany from other countries and live in Nuremberg. It offers support and information. It helps to find the right advice centres or services.
ZAM counselling is a joint service offered by the City of Nuremberg and various welfare organisations. Counselling is available to everyone. Residence status does not matter.
The partners are:

AWO Kreisverband Nürnberg e.V.
Bavarian Red Cross, Nuremberg District Association
Caritas Association Nuremberg e.V.
Johanniter Unfallhilfe e.V.
Nuremberg City Mission e.V.

ZAM-Be offers various counselling services:

By telephone
In writing
Online
In person on site without an appointment

The focus is on German language acquisition.
Learning German is an important area of counselling. ZAM-Be helps you to find the right language course. It also explains how to pay for the course. It also provides support with enrolment.
For people who are not allowed to attend integration courses or vocational language courses, there is the opportunity to take part in city-funded language courses. This takes place as part of the Municipal German Language Acquisition Programme (KPDe) in cooperation with the Education Office of the City of Nuremberg. 
If you need more information, please contact the ZAM counselling service directly:

Contact
                    
                        Zentrale Anlaufstelle Migration (ZAM) – Beratung
                    
                    
                    
                Theresienstraße 18, 90403 Nürnbergzam-beratung@stadt.nuernberg.de+49 91123139215https://www.nuernberg.de/internet/integration/zambe.html
 Open counselling: Monday to Friday from 08:30 to 12:30 (afternoons by appointment)
This project is funded by the Bavarian State Ministry of the Interior, Sport and Integration.
content:
Central Contact Point Migration (ZAM) - Counselling

The "Zentrale Anlaufstelle Migration - Beratung" (ZAM-Be) helps people who have come to Germany from other countries and live in Nuremberg. It offers support and information. It helps to find the right advice centres or services.

ZAM counselling is a joint service offered by the City of Nuremberg and various welfare organisations. Counselling is available to everyone. Residence status does not matter.

The partners are:

  • AWO Kreisverband Nürnberg e.V.
  • Bavarian Red Cross, Nuremberg District Association
  • Caritas Association Nuremberg e.V.
  • Johanniter Unfallhilfe e.V.
  • Nuremberg City Mission e.V.

ZAM-Be offers various counselling services:

  1. By telephone
  2. In writing
  3. Online
  4. In person on site without an appointment

The focus is on German language acquisition.

Learning German is an important area of counselling. ZAM-Be helps you to find the right language course. It also explains how to pay for the course. It also provides support with enrolment.

For people who are not allowed to attend integration courses or vocational language courses, there is the opportunity to take part in city-funded language courses. This takes place as part of the Municipal German Language Acquisition Programme (KPDe) in cooperation with the Education Office of the City of Nuremberg.

If you need more information, please contact the ZAM counselling service directly:

Contact

  • zam-beratung@stadt.nuernberg.de
  • +49 91123139215
  • https://www.nuernberg.de/internet/integration/zambe.html

Open counselling: Monday to Friday from 08:30 to 12:30 (afternoons by appointment)

This project is funded by the Bavarian State Ministry of the Interior, Sport and Integration.

title: Refugee and integration counseling
excerpt:
The advice service helps refugees and asylum seekers. It answers questions. It helps with problems. It guides people through the asylum process and their new daily life.
Even if someone is new to Germany and has been granted permanent residence, they can still contact this advice service.
The Refugee and Integration Advice Service offers support on a range of topics, such as:

Asylum procedures – providing information and answering questions
Providing help and advice on dealing with government departments
Finding local services
Finding your way around the local area
Providing advice and support on everyday problems
Providing advice on integration services (such as vocational integration)
Providing advice on family reunification
How can I support myself?
Referring people to other specialist services
Provide advice on voluntary return or onward migration
You are the point of contact for volunteers. You answer specialist questions about the asylum process

There is a designated Refugee and Integration Advisory Service for every shared accommodation facility or AnKER branch in Nuremberg. 
If you need help or support, please contact the staff at the Refugee and Integration Advice Centre in your shared accommodation or the central advice offices of the Refugee and Integration Advice Centre.
content:
The advice service helps refugees and asylum seekers. It answers questions. It helps with problems. It guides people through the asylum process and their new daily life.

Even if someone is new to Germany and has been granted permanent residence, they can still contact this advice service.


The Refugee and Integration Advice Service offers support on a range of topics, such as:

  • Asylum procedures – providing information and answering questions
  • Providing help and advice on dealing with government departments
  • Finding local services
  • Finding your way around the local area
  • Providing advice and support on everyday problems
  • Providing advice on integration services (such as vocational integration)
  • Providing advice on family reunification
  • How can I support myself?
  • Referring people to other specialist services
  • Provide advice on voluntary return or onward migration
  • You are the point of contact for volunteers. You answer specialist questions about the asylum process

There is a designated Refugee and Integration Advisory Service for every shared accommodation facility or AnKER branch in Nuremberg. 

If you need help or support, please contact the staff at the Refugee and Integration Advice Centre in your shared accommodation or the central advice offices of the Refugee and Integration Advice Centre.

title: Migration Advice Centres for Adults (Ages 27+)
excerpt:
The Migration Advice Centre offers support to all people aged over 27 and who have either a long-term residence permit for Germany; a freedom of movement permit; or right to permanent residence (the keywords here are "recognised refugee"). The advice is given free of charge. There is help, support, information and education available on the following topics:

Child and partner reunification, family reunification
Integration courses/language courses (German)
Recognition of school/university degrees from outside Germany (guidance/initial counselling)
Educational and social insurance systems (information and education)
Authorities, e.g. explanation of decisions/procedures;
Financial support for various things, including help filling out application forms, foundations for non-German national and refugee-specific issues such as residence rights, the Federal Employment Law, and work permits
Living and Working in Germany

There is a dedicated advice centre for teenagers and young adults aged 12 and 27 years old: »»Youth Migration Advice Centres. There is also the »»Asylum Advice Centre, which primarily offers you its support in the local community accommodation centres.

title: Club 402
excerpt:
Under the one roof of the Club 402 Refugee Skills Centre you can find a refugee advice centre run by Gemeinschaftsunterkunft Regensburger Straße, an open child and youth centre, and an intercultural social venue.
These services are available there for you:

Individual and needs-oriented advice sessions
Open support services for children and teenagers

title: North Bavarian Central Repatriation Advice Centre (RZB)
excerpt:
Do you plan on returning to your previous country, before the BAMF has made a decision on your application for asylum? Are you uncertain how to get your passport back or do you need financial help for the journey? The Central Repatriation Advice Centre (ZRB) can advise you on your options on voluntarily returning to your previous country. The advice is provided in an open-ended way. This means that you yourself decide after your advice session whether or not you will voluntarily go to another country.

title: Citizens' offices
excerpt:
If you are a new resident of Nuremberg, you must register. You can do this at one of the citizens' offices in the city of Nuremberg.
You need an appointment to register in Nuremberg. Pleasebook this online(online appointment booking). Under "Meldeangelegenheiten", select "Wohnung anmelden (Zuzug nach Nürnberg)". You will be given the addresses of the citizens' registration offices when you book your appointment online.
All persons aged 16 and over must appear in person for registration. However: If a married couple or a family has come to Nuremberg together, only one of the spouses needs to come to the Citizens' Registration Office. Children under the age of 16 do not need to be present.
The passports of all persons must be available. If there are no passports, an identity card is also sufficient. If you are moving to Germany and are married, please bring your marriage certificate with you. If you have children, please bring their birth certificates. These should be in international form.
You will need these documents for your appointment:

All existing identification documents of all parties involved 
Confirmation of residence from your landlord/landlady (you can download this  here )

 Please bring someone with you to translate if you do not speak or understand German well enough. This is important so that you can understand all the information correctly and make yourself understood.
content:
If you are a new resident of Nuremberg, you must register. You can do this at one of the citizens' offices in the city of Nuremberg.

You need an appointment to register in Nuremberg. Pleasebook this online(online appointment booking). Under "Meldeangelegenheiten", select "Wohnung anmelden (Zuzug nach Nürnberg)". You will be given the addresses of the citizens' registration offices when you book your appointment online.

All persons aged 16 and over must appear in person for registration. However: If a married couple or a family has come to Nuremberg together, only one of the spouses needs to come to the Citizens' Registration Office. Children under the age of 16 do not need to be present.

The passports of all persons must be available. If there are no passports, an identity card is also sufficient. If you are moving to Germany and are married, please bring your marriage certificate with you. If you have children, please bring their birth certificates. These should be in international form.

You will need these documents for your appointment:

  • All existing identification documents of all parties involved
  • Confirmation of residence from your landlord/landlady (you can download this here )

Please bring someone with you to translate if you do not speak or understand German well enough. This is important so that you can understand all the information correctly and make yourself understood.

title: Social Welfare Office (SHA)
excerpt:
When you arrive, you will be given your first appointment at the social welfare office (SHA). There you will be asked about your financial circumstances (for example, whether you have any valuables such as jewellery or money). You will sign some documents and declarations. Please bring all your papers and all the people listed on your "Aufenthaltsgestattung" (residence permit) with you to this appointment. Do you have an interpreter? Bring it with you.
You will receive money for your living expenses. The money will be transferred to your payment card . Please note that the amount must be sufficient for one month. You will normally only receive money from the social welfare office once a month.
Amt für Existenzsicherung und soziale Integration - SozialamtWirtschaftliche Hilfen - Leistungen für Asylbewerber  Frauentorgraben 17, 90443 Nuremberg Monday - Friday 08:30 - 12:30
Nuremberg City Treasury  Theresienstr. 1, 90403 Nuremberg Monday - Friday 08:30 - 12:30
 Appointments are only possible if you have an appointment!
The Social Welfare Office is also your contact for the following topics:


Vouchers for initial baby equipment and for your child's school supplies


Money for train tickets for interview appointments outside of Nuremberg


Sickness certificates, authorisation for operations and other medical measures


""Nuremberg Pass 

""Education and participation package (BUT)

title: The Job Centre
excerpt:
Job Centre is where you go if you are unemployed or need support. It gives you financial support and can also help you to find a job or get qualifications, depending on what you need. Amongst other things, it can help you to find a language course, give you career advice, and help you to get your qualifications recognised in Germany. For more information job advice, support and placements, look here: »»Access to the Job Market.
 If you have a temporary residency permit – meaning that your asylum procedure is still ongoing or your application for asylum has been rejected and you have a certificate granting temporary postponement of deportation (Duldung) – then the »»Employment Agency (Agentur für Arbeit) is where you go for career support.
Steps to Take After a Positive Decision
You have received a positive decision from the BAMF, so the asylum procedure has come to a positive end – in other words, you have been recognised as a refugee. These are the steps you take next:
1. Personal interview

You will have to go to the Job Centre during its opening hours.
You will be registered as a client.
Your job history will be written down.
An advice session will be scheduled. One with the service department and then with the Job Placement Service – so you will have two advisers.
You will be registered as a jobseeker.Job Centre Richard-Wagner-Platz 5, Nürnberg 08:30 – 12:30

2. Administration Department

Important: Advice will only be given if you have an appointment.
Applications for financial support are made during an appointment with your financial support case worker.

3. Job Placement Service

Important: Advice will only be given if you have an appointment.
If you have certificates or letters saying that you are required to attend an integration course or confirming that you have taken part in an integration course, bring them with you.
If you have documents about school attendance, training, work testimonials/reference letters, recognition of qualifications obtained abroad, etc., please bring them with you.
Tell your case worker about any special skills you have (trades, crafts, language skills, etc.).
Before you go, think about what kind of work you want to do.Job Centre South – Job Placement Service Platenstraße 46, 90441 Nürnberg

Note: The staff at the Job Centre are not allowed to pass information about their clients at the Job Centre to anyone else. As a volunteer, a »»Letter of Authorisation (Vollmacht) is required. It must be filled in and signed by both parties.

For more information job advice, support and placements, look here: »»Access to the Job Market.

If you have a temporary residency permit – meaning that your asylum procedure is still ongoing or your application for asylum has been rejected and you have a certificate granting temporary postponement of deportation (Duldung) – then the »»Employment Agency (Agentur für Arbeit) is where you go for career support.

Steps to Take After a Positive Decision

You have received a positive decision from the BAMF, so the asylum procedure has come to a positive end – in other words, you have been recognised as a refugee. These are the steps you take next:

1. Personal interview

  • You will have to go to the Job Centre during its opening hours.
  • You will be registered as a client.
  • Your job history will be written down.
  • An advice session will be scheduled. One with the service department and then with the Job Placement Service – so you will have two advisers.
  • You will be registered as a jobseeker.

    Job Centre
     Richard-Wagner-Platz 5, Nürnberg
     08:30 – 12:30

2. Administration Department

  • Important: Advice will only be given if you have an appointment.
  • Applications for financial support are made during an appointment with your financial support case worker.

3. Job Placement Service

  • Important: Advice will only be given if you have an appointment.
  • If you have certificates or letters saying that you are required to attend an integration course or confirming that you have taken part in an integration course, bring them with you.
  • If you have documents about school attendance, training, work testimonials/reference letters, recognition of qualifications obtained abroad, etc., please bring them with you.
  • Tell your case worker about any special skills you have (trades, crafts, language skills, etc.).
  • Before you go, think about what kind of work you want to do.

    Job Centre South – Job Placement Service
    Platenstraße 46, 90441 Nürnberg

Note: The staff at the Job Centre are not allowed to pass information about their clients at the Job Centre to anyone else. As a volunteer, a »»Letter of Authorisation (Vollmacht) is required. It must be filled in and signed by both parties.

title: Federal Employment Agency (Agentur für Arbeit)
excerpt:
If your asylum procedure is still ongoing or if you are a tolerated foreigner, the Federal Employment Agency is your point of contact for questions concerning the transition from school to work (career guidance), job placement and counselling on continuing vocational training and for employer advice (employer service).
For more information on counselling and placement in the job market, please click here:»»Access to the job market.
 Are you recognised? Then the »»job centre is  your point of contact for counselling and mediation in the job market.

title: Anti-Discrimination Centre
excerpt:
The City of Nuremberg’s Human Rights Office helps people who feel they have been discriminated against. Discrimination means that a person is treated unfairly or excluded. There can be various reasons for this, such as their origin, skin colour, gender or sexual identity. Discrimination can happen anywhere – for example, in everyday life or at work. People may be insulted, verbally abused or treated badly. If something like this happens to you, you can contact the Anti-Discrimination Office. They will be able to help you.

Contact
                    
                        Beauftragter für Diskriminierungsfragen
                    
                    
                    
                Fünferplatz 1, 90403 Nürnbergdiskrimierungsfragen@stadt.nuernberg.de+49 91123110312https://www.nuernberg.de/internet/menschenrechte/antidiskriminierung.html
 Appointments by arrangement

title: B.U.D. - Counselling. Support. Documentation for victims of right-wing extremist violence
excerpt:
B.U.D. is a Bavaria-wide counselling centre for victims of right-wing, racist and anti-Semitic violence. B.U.D. counsels and supports people who have been insulted, threatened or attacked by right-wing extremists or racists, or who have witnessed an attack.
There you can talk about your fears and your emotional situation. B.U.D. will explain the course of an investigation and accompany you to the police and to court if you wish to be accompanied. You will also be helped to find lawyers and therapists.
Counselling is free, confidential and outreach. If there are communication problems, an interpreter will be organised. B.U.D. is independent of state authorities. You decide for yourself how B.U.D. can support you.
B.U.D. - Counselling. Support. Documentation for victims of right-wing extremist violence info@bud-bayern.de 0151 / 21653187 www.bud-bayern.de

There you can talk about your fears and your emotional situation. B.U.D. will explain the course of an investigation and accompany you to the police and to court if you wish to be accompanied. You will also be helped to find lawyers and therapists.

title: Become active as a volunteer
excerpt:
Volunteering in Nuremberg
Many people in Germany do voluntary work. This means that they work voluntarily in their free time because they enjoy it. These people don't get paid for it.  They also say: get involved. This means working for other people or for a cause. Anyone can work as much as they want. You have to speak German well for almost all voluntary work. For some activities, speaking German is not so important. In Nuremberg, over 100,000 people do voluntary work without being paid.
There are many opportunities to do voluntary work. For example, you can

help older people
help look after children
work as a sports coach in a sports club
support people with disabilities
work as a paramedic
Helping refugees
help at an animal shelter
and many other things

Volunteering during the asylum procedure in Nuremberg
Even if you are still in the asylum procedure, you can do voluntary work.  As a rule, you do not need permission from the Foreigners' Registration Office to do voluntary work.
However, there are exceptions! You can only take up the so-called "Federal Voluntary Service" if you are recognised as entitled to asylum and have a work permit from the responsible immigration authority.
For some voluntary activities (but not all!) you will receive money. This amount is called an "honorary allowance". If you receive benefits in accordance with the AsylbLG, you may receive up to 200 euros per month free of charge. This means that this amount of money will not be deducted from your benefits. Even if you are already recognised, i.e. receive benefits from the job centre, you may keep up to 200 euros per month without your benefits being reduced.It is best to enquire where you would like to do voluntary work as to whether there is an allowance for voluntary work.
Free advice
If you would like to volunteer in Nuremberg and already speak German well, you can get advice. Free coun selling takes place twice a week. Anyone can come!
Klara - Centre for Advice and Engagement Klaragasse 3, 90402 Nuremberg  Every Wednesday 11:00 - 14:00 Every Thursday 16:00 - 18:00
People who do not yet speak German well can register here:
Centre for Active Citizens 0911 9297170 or email zab@iska-nuernberg.de www.iska-nuernberg.de/zab/
Search on the Internet
You can also search the internet. There is a site with many job opportunities for people who would like to do voluntary work. The name of the site is Bürgernetz. There you can choose in which field you would like to work. For example, "Family", "Sport" or "Life in old age". But there are many more areas. Perhaps you would also like to volunteer near your home. Then simply type the last two digits of the postcode of your place of residence into the field that already contains 904. All postcodes beginning with 904 are in Nuremberg.
Nuremberg Citizens' Network  www.buergernetz.nuernberg.de
Information on Facebook
You can also find interesting information about volunteering in Nuremberg on the Facebook page"Nürnberg Engagiert".
content:
Volunteering in Nuremberg

Many people in Germany do voluntary work. This means that they work voluntarily in their free time because they enjoy it. These people don't get paid for it. They also say: get involved. This means working for other people or for a cause. Anyone can work as much as they want. You have to speak German well for almost all voluntary work. For some activities, speaking German is not so important. In Nuremberg, over 100,000 people do voluntary work without being paid.

There are many opportunities to do voluntary work. For example, you can

  • help older people
  • help look after children
  • work as a sports coach in a sports club
  • support people with disabilities
  • work as a paramedic
  • Helping refugees
  • help at an animal shelter
  • and many other things

Volunteering during the asylum procedure in Nuremberg

Even if you are still in the asylum procedure, you can do voluntary work. As a rule, you do not need permission from the Foreigners' Registration Office to do voluntary work.

However, there are exceptions! You can only take up the so-called "Federal Voluntary Service" if you are recognised as entitled to asylum and have a work permit from the responsible immigration authority.

For some voluntary activities (but not all!) you will receive money. This amount is called an "honorary allowance". If you receive benefits in accordance with the AsylbLG, you may receive up to 200 euros per month free of charge. This means that this amount of money will not be deducted from your benefits. Even if you are already recognised, i.e. receive benefits from the job centre, you may keep up to 200 euros per month without your benefits being reduced.
It is best to enquire where you would like to do voluntary work as to whether there is an allowance for voluntary work.

Free advice

If you would like to volunteer in Nuremberg and already speak German well, you can get advice. Free coun selling takes place twice a week. Anyone can come!

Klara - Centre for Advice and Engagement
Klaragasse 3, 90402 Nuremberg
Every Wednesday 11:00 - 14:00
Every Thursday 16:00 - 18:00

People who do not yet speak German well can register here:

Centre for Active Citizens
0911 9297170 or email zab@iska-nuernberg.de
www.iska-nuernberg.de/zab/

Search on the Internet

You can also search the internet. There is a site with many job opportunities for people who would like to do voluntary work. The name of the site is Bürgernetz. There you can choose in which field you would like to work. For example, "Family", "Sport" or "Life in old age". But there are many more areas. Perhaps you would also like to volunteer near your home. Then simply type the last two digits of the postcode of your place of residence into the field that already contains 904. All postcodes beginning with 904 are in Nuremberg.

Nuremberg Citizens' Network
www.buergernetz.nuernberg.de

Information on Facebook

You can also find interesting information about volunteering in Nuremberg on the Facebook page"Nürnberg Engagiert".

title: Integration guides
excerpt:
There are integration guides in Bavaria. They support volunteers in order to strengthen integration in Bavaria. Volunteers can contact these people with questions about integration and asylum. In Nuremberg, Katharina Schwartz and Elina Schnürer will be happy to help you.
Every month there is a network meeting on integration and commitment. All interested and committed people can exchange information about living together in Nuremberg. Current topics are discussed together and solutions are sought.
You can find all information here.

You can find all information here.

title: Helper Circles
excerpt:
In many districts, there are groups that volunteer by offering to help you free of charge. The volunteers can help you to find your bearings in your new home environment, participate in leisure and sport activities, and learn your first German language skills. If you have questions about daily life, you can ask the volunteers. They can assist in many ways, such as helping you to understand a letter written in German, letting you know where there are cheap shopping options and sport facilities, or telling you how the bus system works. The volunteers can also help you with opening a bank account. Many Helper Circles also offer assistance when it comes to learning German. You can contact the Helper Circles via email.
Here is a list of all the Helper Circles in Nuremberg:»»Nuremberg Helper Circles

title: Helpful Links
excerpt:
Helpful Links
For Your Asylum Hearing

An information leaflet about the asylum procedure has been put together by the Integration Association for Asylum and Migration:  »»The Asylum Procedure: The Hearing 
The Cologne Refugee Council has published a preparation video for asylum applicants' BAMF hearings. It is available in 14 languages: »»Asylum in Germany. 
Download the»»Ankommen app. It is a useful guide for your first few weeks in Germany.

Life in Germany

The German journalist Konstantin Schreiber has put together some 5-minute videos in which she discusses – in Arabic with German subtitles – everyday topics about life in Germany:

 www.n-tv.de/marhaba/

The Refugee Law Clinic Erlangen-Nuremberg e.V. offers free legal advice on the asylum procedure. To get an appointment, you need to register in advance by email.

title: First Steps - Integration Centre for Refugees
excerpt:
The First Steps team supports refugees and new arrivals as they take their first steps, for example through discussion groups, German language courses, IT courses and evening sessions for young men. These services are free of charge.

title: Help for people from Ukraine
Information for Ukrainian refugees in Nuremberg
excerpt:
Welcome to Nuremberg!
Do you need support? Then you can contact these centres:
content:
Welcome to Nuremberg!

Do you need support? Then you can contact these centres:

title: Social benefits
excerpt:
Refugees from Ukraine who are fit for work are entitled, from 1 June 2022, to benefits under the Second Book of the Social Code (SGB II) – also known as Unemployment Benefit II or Hartz IV. Provided you hold a valid residence permit or a valid provisional residence certificate, the Nuremberg City Jobcentre has been responsible for refugees from Ukraine since 1 June 2022.
Refugees from Ukraine in need of assistance who have reached the German state pension age – that is, those born before 1 July 1956 – are entitled to benefits under the Basic Security in Old Age and in the Event of Reduced Earning Capacity scheme under Book XII of the Social Code (SGB XII). Those eligible for benefits under SGB XII will continue to receive financial support from the social welfare office.
The following infographic shows, step by step, where and how refugees can apply for income support:Infographic – How do I receive benefits if I arrived in Nuremberg from Ukraine after 31 May 2022 and am in need of assistance?
 
Eligibility criteria for benefits under SGB II (Citizen’s Income)
To receive the citizen’s income, two things are essential:

A residence permit or a provisional residence certificate, and
a fully completed application for Bürgergeld

You can receive Bürgergeld provided you meet the requirements. Bürgergeld covers money for day-to-day living, the costs of rent and heating, and the costs of statutory health insurance. In addition, the Jobcentre offers further benefits, such as advice and job placement, further training opportunities, integration and language courses, and higher financial benefits. 
Applying 
The best way to apply for your Bürgergeld is online at www.jobcenter.digital. You can use this portal to sort out almost all matters with the Jobcentre, saving you a lot of time and effort.Alternatively, you can submit your application here.  
You can also visit us in person. Our address is: ‘ Richard-Wagner-Platz 5 ’ in Nuremberg. This is right next to the Opera House.You can collect and submit your application there. We are open Monday to Friday from 8.30 am to 12.00 pm.
If you have any further questions, please contact the Nuremberg City Jobcentre:
   09 11 / 40 07 10 0 (Monday–Friday, 08:00–18:00)
Please bear in mind that we can only advise you in person in emergencies, or if you have booked an appointment in advance.
Further information

Don’t have a provisional residence certificate yet? If you have any questions, you can find out more here.
Information on accommodation costs under SGB II can be found here.
On the uahelp website, you’ll find all the information in your own language!
content:
Refugees from Ukraine who are fit for work are entitled, from 1 June 2022, to benefits under the Second Book of the Social Code (SGB II) – also known as Unemployment Benefit II or Hartz IV. Provided you hold a valid residence permit or a valid provisional residence certificate, the Nuremberg City Jobcentre has been responsible for refugees from Ukraine since 1 June 2022.

Refugees from Ukraine in need of assistance who have reached the German state pension age – that is, those born before 1 July 1956 – are entitled to benefits under the Basic Security in Old Age and in the Event of Reduced Earning Capacity scheme under Book XII of the Social Code (SGB XII). Those eligible for benefits under SGB XII will continue to receive financial support from the social welfare office.

The following infographic shows, step by step, where and how refugees can apply for income support:Infographic – How do I receive benefits if I arrived in Nuremberg from Ukraine after 31 May 2022 and am in need of assistance?

 

Eligibility criteria for benefits under SGB II (Citizen’s Income)

To receive the citizen’s income, two things are essential:

  • A residence permit or a provisional residence certificate, and
  • a fully completed application for Bürgergeld

You can receive Bürgergeld provided you meet the requirements. Bürgergeld covers money for day-to-day living, the costs of rent and heating, and the costs of statutory health insurance. In addition, the Jobcentre offers further benefits, such as advice and job placement, further training opportunities, integration and language courses, and higher financial benefits. 

Applying

The best way to apply for your Bürgergeld is online at www.jobcenter.digital. You can use this portal to sort out almost all matters with the Jobcentre, saving you a lot of time and effort.
Alternatively, you can submit your application here.  


You can also visit us in person. Our address is: ‘ Richard-Wagner-Platz 5 ’ in Nuremberg. This is right next to the Opera House.
You can collect and submit your application there. We are open Monday to Friday from 8.30 am to 12.00 pm.

If you have any further questions, please contact the Nuremberg City Jobcentre:

   09 11 / 40 07 10 0 (Monday–Friday, 08:00–18:00)

Please bear in mind that we can only advise you in person in emergencies, or if you have booked an appointment in advance.

Further information

  • Don’t have a provisional residence certificate yet? If you have any questions, you can find out more here.
  • Information on accommodation costs under SGB II can be found here.
  • On the uahelp website, you’ll find all the information in your own language!

title: Registration at schools
excerpt:
Are you looking for a school?
This flyer provides information about enrolment at the school: Download - Flyer bilingual.
On the website of the education authority you will find information about primary and secondary schools in Nuremberg. The site is for schools, parents and teachers. There you will find information on registration, counselling, German classes, events and current topics: https://nbg.schulamt.info/.
Information on enrolment at a vocational school can be found on this page: www.nuernberg.de/internet/berufsschule_5.
Pupils can find information on registration, lessons and counselling on the website. Current dates are also listed there. The school provides information about environmental and sustainability projects and organises events such as fashion shows. The school focusses on diversity, integration and good practical training.

title: Creative workshop, painting and handicrafts
excerpt:
The offers are generally free of charge and usually do not require registration. If costs are incurred for individual offers or registration is required, we will provide this information in the description text of the respective offer. If you have any questions, please contact the respective organiser directly or discuss them on site.
---
(#1)Creative offer for (Ukrainian/refugee) families - free design Participants are invited to choose their own materials to work with. There are large sheets of paper, chalks and coloured pencils, wet paint, clay and much more. The participants primarily decide what they want to work on, but some creative ideas are also offered.Children aged 8 and over can also come on their own.
Protestant Family Education Centre (FBS) Tuesday 14:30 - 16:30  Creative room U2 in the FBS, Leonhardstraße 13, 90443 Nuremberg 0911 2747660  info@fbs-nuernberg.de  www.fbs-nuernberg.de
 
(#3)Family creative day Children of all ages and their parents, grandparents or other accompanying persons are welcome to attend the family creative day on the last Sunday of every month. Each family works on their own creative island. There is a colourful box of materials with inspiration for individual or joint projects. In between, you can stack daring towers, build fantastic cities or create colourful pixel pictures in your building corner with thousands of colourful wooden bricks.
Children's art room  Last Sunday of the month, 14:00 - 16:00 Fürther Straße 244d, 90429 Nuremberg  0911 23114816  www.kinderkunstraum.nuernberg.de
 
(#4)Art support for refugee children and adults with Nadiya Pankova An open painting course for refugee children, adults and families. I would like to support both the children and their mothers. During this time, the children should be able to better process what they have seen and experienced through painting, distract themselves from the negative events and focus on the positive. While the children are being looked after, the adults can concentrate on organising everyday life in Germany.The workshop leader is Nadiya Pankova, who herself fled Donetsk in 2014 and has been living in Germany since 2017. She speaks Ukrainian, Russian, German and English. The children can choose a language.
Community centre Langwasser  Children: Tuesday, 13:00 - 15:00; Adults: Tuesday, 19:15 - 21:30 Glogauer Str. 50, 90473 Nuremberg  0911 23115700 www.nuernberg.de/internet/kuf_kultur/gemeinschaftshaus_langwasser.html

Germanic National Museum Saturdays, 13:00 - 16:00 and every first Saturday of the month for everyone, no age limit  Kartäusergasse 1, 90402 Nuremberg  0911 1331245 p.schroeder.kpz@gnm.de www.gnm.de www.kpz-nuernberg.de

Generations and cultures meet here to exchange ideas about the most beautiful hobby in the world and let the needles rattle together! Anyone who enjoys knitting is welcome. So pack your needles and come to our intercultural & intergenerational knitting get-together! You generally have to bring your own knitting projects. The offers are generally free of charge and usually do not require registration. (#8) Piano practiceThere is a slightly smaller piano in the multi-generation house. If anyone wants to practise the piano, they are welcome to make an appointment with us. Registration under 0911 45060167.

(#61)Field of experience for the development of the sensesFocus topic: Man and natureTarget group of the offer Families, children's groups from 5 years of ageAdmission prices as with Nuremberg Pass:Children from 3 years: € 4.00Adults: € 4.75Groups of 10 or more: 1 to 2 accompanying persons free of charge
Field of experience in general:The Field of Experience for the Development of the Senses is an "action trail for sensory perception", which offers tasks, games, exercises, experiments and information on the individual senses at the individual stations. The Nuremberg Field of Experience is characterised by its annually changing focus. The 100 or so experience stations are complemented by workshops and an extensive supporting programme.Field of Experience 2022:With the motto "Man and Nature", the Field of Experience will open its doors again on 1 May 2022 on the Wöhrder Wiese. This season, everything revolves around sustainability, the environment, climate and how people can live in harmony with nature. New experience stations, which focus on the considerate and responsible treatment of our planet, take a look at the relationship between humans and nature.Once again this year, more than 100 adventure stations invite visitors to marvel, discover and try things out in the midst of city and meadow dwellers. The spirit of research and thirst for action are playfully fuelled here.Thematically appropriate classics such as a baking station and bee tent are complemented by a tree course, body percussion with "Man - Nature - Rhythm", an environmental laboratory ... and these are by no means all the new stations. And the best thing is: everything can be tried out.
Office for Culture and Leisure KUF 1 May to 11 September, daily 14:00 - 18:00  Field of experience of the senses, Wöhrder Wiese  0911 2315445 erfahrungsfeld@stadt.nuernberg.de www.nuernberg.de/internet/kuf_kultur/erfahrungsfeld.html

The offers are generally free of charge and usually do not require registration. If costs are incurred for individual offers or registration is required, we will provide this information in the description text of the respective offer. If you have any questions, please contact the respective organiser directly or discuss them on site.

---

(#8)
Piano practice
There is a slightly smaller piano in the multi-generation house. If anyone wants to practise the piano, they are welcome to make an appointment with us. Registration under 0911 45060167.

Multi-generation centre AWOthek
by appointment
Karl-Bröger-Straße 9, 90459 Nuremberg
0911 45060167
MGH-Assistenz@awo-nbg.de
www.mgh-awothek.de

(#61)
Field of experience for the development of the senses
Focus topic: Man and nature
Target group of the offer Families, children's groups from 5 years of age
Admission prices as with Nuremberg Pass:
Children from 3 years: € 4.00
Adults: € 4.75
Groups of 10 or more: 1 to 2 accompanying persons free of charge

Field of experience in general:
The Field of Experience for the Development of the Senses is an "action trail for sensory perception", which offers tasks, games, exercises, experiments and information on the individual senses at the individual stations. The Nuremberg Field of Experience is characterised by its annually changing focus. The 100 or so experience stations are complemented by workshops and an extensive supporting programme.
Field of Experience 2022:
With the motto "Man and Nature", the Field of Experience will open its doors again on 1 May 2022 on the Wöhrder Wiese. This season, everything revolves around sustainability, the environment, climate and how people can live in harmony with nature. New experience stations, which focus on the considerate and responsible treatment of our planet, take a look at the relationship between humans and nature.
Once again this year, more than 100 adventure stations invite visitors to marvel, discover and try things out in the midst of city and meadow dwellers. The spirit of research and thirst for action are playfully fuelled here.
Thematically appropriate classics such as a baking station and bee tent are complemented by a tree course, body percussion with "Man - Nature - Rhythm", an environmental laboratory ... and these are by no means all the new stations. And the best thing is: everything can be tried out.

Office for Culture and Leisure KUF
1 May to 11 September, daily 14:00 - 18:00
Field of experience of the senses, Wöhrder Wiese
0911 2315445
erfahrungsfeld@stadt.nuernberg.de
www.nuernberg.de/internet/kuf_kultur/erfahrungsfeld.html

Gardeningwith Ukrainian children
