title: Educational programmes, German language
excerpt:
In the flyers you will find information on access to language courses for people who have fled from Ukraine in German, Ukrainian and Russian.
---
The courses are generally free of charge and usually do not require registration. If costs are incurred for individual offers or registration is required, we will provide this information in the description text of the respective offer. If you have any questions, please contact the respective organiser directly or discuss them on site.
---
 
(#33)Maths for children aged 10 to 12The language of the programme is Russian. Registration required at the telephone number below!
Cultural workshop at AEG Monday, 10:00 - 10:50 a.m.  Kulturwerkstatt auf AEG, Fürther Str. 244d,90429 Nuremberg; U1 stop Eberhardshof  0176 61110684www.nuernberg.de/internet/kuf_kultur/kulturwerkstatt_wirueberuns.html
 
(#35)Maths for children aged 13 to 15The language of the programme is Russian. Registration required at the telephone number below!
Cultural workshop at AEG Monday, 11:00 - 12:00  Kulturwerkstatt auf AEG, Fürther Str. 244d,90429 Nuremberg; U1 stop Eberhardshof  0176 61110684www.nuernberg.de/internet/kuf_kultur/kulturwerkstatt_wirueberuns.html
 
(#47)Language and learning caféAt the NOA language café, you can learn your first words and sentences in German with teachers and volunteers, practise grammar, talk about pictures and texts or prepare for an exam.
 NOA Monday and Friday 14:00 - 16:00; Tuesday and Thursday 9:00 - 11:00   Siebenkeestraße 4; 90459 Nuremberg (underground station Aufseßplatz)  0911 58630  sabine_winkler@noa.nuernberg.de   www.noa-nuernberg.de/
 
(#48) Using the PC and digital learning
 NOA Monday, 9:00 - 11:00 a.m., Tuesday, 2:00 - 4:00 p.m.   Language café in the Lernwerkstatt, Allersberger Straße 130, 90461 Nuremberg (2nd floor)  0911 58630  sabine_winkler@noa.nuernberg.de   www.noa-nuernberg.de/
 
(#76)Learning café SJD - Die FalkenLearning café at the Falkentreff AMIKARO in Gostenhof for children and young people aged 10 and over. We cook, play, do handicrafts and do homework together.
Falcon office Thursday, 14:00 - 17:00 and once a month on Tuesdays 14:00-17:00 for Minta*/girls AMIKARO, Adam-Klein-Str. 20 in 90429 Nuremberg 0911 443709 buero@falken-nuernberg.de  www.stadtteilforum.org/einrichtungen/einrichtungen/show/amikaro-falkentreff.html
 
(#81)Project: DRUZIWith the "DRUZI" project, we want to bring a bit of normality into the lives of those who have had to leave their homes, families and friends, their school life and hobbies all at once. A total of 25 children from Ukraine aged between 5 and 15 will take part in our project. The project includes lessons and workshops and takes place weekly from Monday to Friday. There will also be a weekend programme later on.
Luise - The Cultfactory Monday to Friday, 10:00 - 15:00   Scharrerstraße 15, 90478 Nuremberg 0176 20751849  e.rusliakova@gmail.com  www.luise-cultfactory.de/
 
(#86)Online German coursesFor all those who can and want to learn online, the "Nürnberger Haus" offers online German courses at various levels. Due to the war, the course instructors are scattered and only some of them are still in Kharkiv. Nevertheless, the team spirit is there. The programme is aimed equally at Ukrainian refugees in Nuremberg and those who have remained in Ukraine. Registration required via the link provided.
Nuremberg House in Kharkiv different (see registration forms) Online registration   Registration for A1 at this link Registration for A2-B2 at this link
 
 
(#102)Signpost courses: Living in GermanyInformation course in Russian "Life in Germany".Contents are:- Society, basics of living together in Germany- Education for children and adults in Germany- Medicine, doctors, medication- Everyday life: money, bank, insurance, housing, transport, shopping etc.- Opportunities to learn German.

title: Everyday life
excerpt: Are you new in Germany?! Moving to a foreign country brings with it a lot of questions and a lot of work. Everyday life or some German ways of life must be foreign to many refugees and migrants. In order for it to be a little easier to get around in your everyday life, here are a few practical tips.
content: Are you new in Germany?! Moving to a foreign country brings with it a lot of questions and a lot of work. Everyday life or some German ways of life must be foreign to many refugees and migrants. In order for it to be a little easier to get around in your everyday life, here are a few practical tips.

title: Work opportunities (AGH) for people entitled to benefits under the Asylum Seekers Benefits Act
excerpt:
What are work opportunities?
Work opportunities in accordance with Section 5 of the Asylum Seekers' Benefits Act (AsylbLG) are intended to promote integration into society. At the same time, they make it possible to earn money independently. Occupational health and safety regulations apply and there is accident insurance cover.
The work opportunity is not an employment relationship subject to social security contributions.
A work permit is not required.
 
Who can take up a work opportunity?
Recipients of benefits under the Asylum Seekers Benefits Act (not under SGB II, Job Centre) who are able to work and are not of compulsory school age.
 
What activities are possible?
The activities can be carried out with state, municipal or non-profit organisations.
Examples:

Clearing weeds and leaves or cleaning paths,
cleaning rooms and buildings,
helping elderly people or helping others with translations.

If you need special clothing, such as helmets or sturdy shoes, these are provided by the provider.
 
How many hours can the work opportunities last?
You can work a maximum of 20 hours per week.
 
How much is the payment?
You get 80 cents for every hour. The social welfare office pays the extra money by posting it to the payment card.
 Do I have to do a work opportunity?
Anyone who wants to volunteer can register.
Anyone who has registered is then obliged to do the work for a certain period of time. Then the work must be done until the agreed end. Only if you fall ill or have another important reason can you stop doing the work before then. 
If you simply stop without a good reason, you will receive less money from the social welfare office (benefits under the Asylum Seekers' Benefits Act (AsylbLG) may be reduced).
 
Are you interested in AGH?
Then talk to your asylum social counselling service in your accommodation.
 All information can be found here.
content:
What are work opportunities?

Work opportunities in accordance with Section 5 of the Asylum Seekers' Benefits Act (AsylbLG) are intended to promote integration into society. At the same time, they make it possible to earn money independently. Occupational health and safety regulations apply and there is accident insurance cover.

The work opportunity is not an employment relationship subject to social security contributions.

A work permit is not required.

Who can take up a work opportunity?

Recipients of benefits under the Asylum Seekers Benefits Act (not under SGB II, Job Centre) who are able to work and are not of compulsory school age.

What activities are possible?

The activities can be carried out with state, municipal or non-profit organisations.

Examples:

  • Clearing weeds and leaves or cleaning paths,
  • cleaning rooms and buildings,
  • helping elderly people or helping others with translations.

If you need special clothing, such as helmets or sturdy shoes, these are provided by the provider.

How many hours can the work opportunities last?

You can work a maximum of 20 hours per week.

How much is the payment?

You get 80 cents for every hour. The social welfare office pays the extra money by posting it to the payment card.


Do I have to do a work opportunity?

Anyone who wants to volunteer can register.

Anyone who has registered is then obliged to do the work for a certain period of time. Then the work must be done until the agreed end. Only if you fall ill or have another important reason can you stop doing the work before then.

If you simply stop without a good reason, you will receive less money from the social welfare office (benefits under the Asylum Seekers' Benefits Act (AsylbLG) may be reduced).

Are you interested in AGH?

Then talk to your asylum social counselling service in your accommodation.

All information can be found here.

title: Payment card
excerpt:
What is a payment card?

The payment card is like a bank card. 
Benefits under the Asylum Seekers' Benefits Act (AsylbLG) are transferred to the payment card as a credit balance.
You can use the payment card to pay in shops and withdraw cash.
You can only spend as much money as is on the card.

 You can also find information about the payment card in several languages here.
Who gets a payment card?

All persons aged 14 and over who receive benefits under the Asylum Seekers' Benefits Act (AsylbLG) receive a payment card. 
In benefit communities (e.g. families), each family member aged 14 and over receives their own payment card. 

Where can I get my payment card?

If you receive benefits, the social welfare office will send you a letter with all the information and an appointment to collect it.
You don't have to do anything else to get the payment card.
If you are applying for benefits for the first time and they are approved, you will receive a payment card right from the start.

Which benefits go onto the payment card?

Benefits under the Asylum Seekers' Benefits Act (AsylbLG) are paid onto the payment card

 Do you receive other benefits (e.g. child benefit) or wages from an employer? Then you also need a current account.
How much money do I have on the card?

Benefits under the Asylum Seekers' Benefits Act (AsylbLG) are paid onto the payment card.
You can view your balance online at meine.bezahlkarte.eu and in the payment card app.
You will need your card ID and PIN to do this.

 You will find the card ID and PIN in the letter you receive with your payment card.
Where can I use the payment card?

You can pay with the payment card in all shops that accept Mastercard. 

 Look out for the orange / red Mastercard® symbol.
Are there any problems with certain transactions?

Yes, certain transactions are not possible.
For example, you cannot use the payment card with money transfer services such as Western Union, MoneyGram or PayPal.
In special cases, contributions (e.g. for mobile phone contracts, VAG or Deutsche Bahn) can be transferred or collected if you provide the necessary proof in good time.

Can I withdraw cash from the payment card?

Yes, you can withdraw cash.
Each person in your benefit community can withdraw up to €50.00 per month.
You can withdraw cash free of charge from ATMs or at the till in many shops. 
Withdrawals are only possible twice a month.
After that, the card is blocked for further withdrawals.

I have lost my payment card. What do I have to do?

You can block your payment card online at my.paymentcard.eu.
You can also block the card by telephone at  116116 . This is possible in German or English.
If you find the card again, you can reactivate it online at my.paymentcard.eu.

 Please note that the card is not unblocked by the Social Welfare Office (e.g. due to an incorrect PIN entry), but by the PayCentre!
Where can I get help?

There is a support chat online at meine.bezahlkarte.eu and in the payment card app. This is available in several languages. 
A telephone bot is also always available for you in over 100 languages.  +49 (0) 81619654300

title: Public Holidays and Opening Times
excerpt:
Most major food shops in Nuremberg are open from Monday to Saturday from 08:00 to 20:00. Most shops in the city centre are open from 10:00 to 20:00. In smaller shops these times are often shorter. Sometimes there is also a lunch break. On Sundays almost all shops are closed. Except at the central station, where some shops are open.
On a public holiday people don’t work and all shops are closed. There are public holidays across Germany and holidays that vary from state to state. Bavaria has 13 public holidays. Most of them are ecclesiastical.
Buses and trains run at different times on Sundays and public holidays!
Public holidays 2024 in Nuremberg
JanuaryMonday 01 January 2024 (New Year)Saturday 06 January 2024 (Epiphany)MarchFriday, 29  March 2024 (Good Friday)AprilMonday 01 April 2024 (Easter Monday)MayWednesday 01 May 2024 (Labour Day)Thursday 09 May 2024 (Ascension Day)Monday 20 May 2024 (Whit Monday)Thursday 30 May 2024 (Ascension Day)AugustThursday 15 August 2024 (Assumption Day)OctoberThursday 03 October 2024 (German Unity Day)NovemberFriday 01 November 2024 (All Saints’ Day)DecemberWednesday 25 December 2024 (Christmas Day)Thursday 26 December 2024 (Boxing Day)

title: Lost property office
excerpt:
Have you lost your keys or wallet? Perhaps it has been handed in to the lost property office. Lost property is kept there for some time.  If you have found an item, you should also hand it in to the lost property office or the ""Citizens' Information Centre. Use the ""online search.
Lost property office of the city of Nuremberg  Löffelholzstraße 21, 90441 Nuremberg 0911 810097-70  Monday, Tuesday 09:30 - 15:30 Wednesday closed Thursday 09:30 - 18:00 Friday 09:30 - 12:30 www.fundbuero-nuernberg.de
Several times a year there is a lost property bazaar. Items that have not been collected are auctioned off there. Further information can be found on the homepage of the lost property office.

title: Getting around
excerpt: All relevant places in Nuremberg and the surrounding areas can be easily reached by public transport (bus, tram and underground). A healthy, economical and environmentally friendly alternative to getting from A to B is by bicycle.
content: All relevant places in Nuremberg and the surrounding areas can be easily reached by public transport (bus, tram and underground). A healthy, economical and environmentally friendly alternative to getting from A to B is by bicycle.

title: Public transport
excerpt:
Public transport is available so that you can get where you want to go in Nuremberg. The options are bus, tram and underground. In order to move around Nuremberg, there are regional trains (R-Bahn) and the city railway (S-Bahn). For other routes, there are long distance trains and long distance buses.
A timetable for Nuremberg and the region can be found at any stop, on the internet at »»www.vgn.de or on the VGN app.
The VAG has summarised all important information in a multilingual flyer: »»Welcome to Nuremberg with the VAG.
Important: Travelling without a ticket, or dodging the fare, will be punished! A valid ticket is required for each trip. Otherwise you pay a penalty of €60.

You can buy a ticket at the ticket machine. Ticket machines can be found at every underground and suburban railway station. You can also buy a ticket from the driver on the bus and the VAG customer centre at the main station.
If you use public transport more frequently, you can find out about cheaper tickets. For example, you can buy a T10 ticket, a day ticket or a monthly ticket. With the »»Nuremberg pass you can also get a monthly ticket at a reduced price.
You do not have to pay full price for children. Children up to and including 5 years old can travel for free and there are discounted tickets for children between 6 and 14 years old.
You can also change between several means of transport (bus, underground, suburban train) with a single ticket. Exception: Short distance (underground: 2 stations, suburban train and bus: 4 stops)

title: Bicycle
excerpt:
If you know that you will be living in Nuremberg for a longer period, it is worth buying a bike. This is cheaper than the underground, bus and tram.
Important road rules for cyclists (selection)


Always cycle on the right side of the road


Do not cycle side by side but rather one behind the other


If there is a cycle path, you must ride along the cycle path (only on the right side of the road in the direction of travel)


Only children up to 11 years old are allowed to cycle on footpaths


It is dangerous if you end up on the tram lines with your tyres

It is forbidden to cycle while using a mobile phone.

Further information: www.adfc-muenchen.de/adfc-muenchen/arbeitsgruppen/asyl/
Purchase and repairs
When buying, make sure that your bike is fitted with the following safety equipment. Otherwise, you will have to pay a fine when subject to a police check:


Light at the front and rear


Reflector at the front and rear


Reflectors in the spokes (2 per wheel)


Reflectors on the pedals


Bell


Two independent brakes


Addresses of used bicycle shops and repair workshops
Open bike workshop in the Künstlerhaus
Carry out all bike repairs by yourself with guidance. Some used parts are available. Please bring your own (cost €2.50).
Artists’ House in the KunstKulturQuartier Köngstreue 93, 90402 Nuremberg betriebsbuero.kukuq@stadt.nuernberg.de Thursday 18:00 - 20:30 www.kunstkulturquartier.de/kuenstlerhaus/programm/offene-werkstaetten/
Mobility flea market
Every year in the spring, a mobility flea market takes place at three locations in Nuremberg. If you are looking for a cheap bike, the "Mobifloh" is exactly the right place to find one!
The exact dates are provided here: www.intelligent-mobil.nuernberg.de
NOA-Fahrradwerkstatt
There you will find used and new bicycles.
Noa-Fahrradwerkstatt Gutenstetter Straße 20, 90449 Nuremberg +49 (0) 911 5863483/74 fahrradwerkstatt@noa.nuernberg.de Monday - Friday 10:00 - 17:00
More addresses
You can find a bicycle dealer directory on the website of the»»ADFC Nürnberg.

title: Personal liability insurance
excerpt:
Even if you cause damage by accident, you will have to pay for it. This applies, for example, if someone is injured or if something gets broken. For instance: you cause a car accident. Or your child kicks a ball through a window. In such cases, you will have to pay for the damage.
In Germany, you can take out personal liability insurance to cover such damages for you and your family or children. It is up to you to decide whether you wish to take out this insurance. However, we strongly recommend that you do so. Your support group can help you find a good deal on personal liability insurance.
Further information is available at:
 www.verbraucherzentral.de

In Germany, you can take out personal liability insurance to cover such damages for you and your family or children. It is up to you to decide whether you wish to take out this insurance. However, we strongly recommend that you do so. Your support group can help you find a good deal on personal liability insurance.

Further information is available at:

www.verbraucherzentral.de

title: Internet/Wi-fi
excerpt:
As an asylum seeker in Germany you unfortunately have no legal right to internet access (Wi-Fi) in your accommodation. That is why there is normally no Wi-Fi in the accommodation.
If there is a telephone connection in the accommodation, it is possible that you can sign up for a Wi-Fi contract. Ask your help centre to advise you in choosing a good contract. Speak with the people living in the accommodation about the payment. Consider whether a contract that you can always cancel is better than a contract that applies for a long time (for example 2 years).
Important: Talk to your accommodation management first and ask before entering into a contract, whether or not Wi-Fi is technically possible and how you can give the technician access to the infrastructure.
Public, free Wi-Fi hotspots in Nuremberg
Free Wi-Fi is available in several locations (hotspots) in Nuremberg. There you can surf the internet with your own device.

title: Current account (Girokonto)
excerpt:
A current account is an account for people who want to make payments via the bank. Your money is available in your current account at any time.
With a current account you can:

transfer money,
receive cashless payments (disbursements from agencies/authorities (social benefits) are often made cashless, in other words, transferred to a personal current account),
set up standing orders,
use direct debiting,
cash a cheque, pay cashless with an EC/Maestro card,
withdraw money at the bank counter or an ATM
and print your bank statements.

Important:Make sure that you always have money in your bank account.
Tip: Different banks offer current accounts with different conditions. Find out exactly how much a current account costs each month and what services the bank offers.
Opening an account
Decide which bank you want to have an account with. Make an appointment to open the account. You must bring the confirmation from Social Services and your ID or your proof of arrival to the appointment. Please bring one of your identity documents with you: Proof of arrival, residence permit, ID card.If no current address is recorded on the identity document, please provide a registration certificate or similar document.
Important: If you do not speak German or English, please take an interpreter along with you.
Important to note:

Once your bank account has been opened, you will receive an EC debit card and a four-digit PIN code.Sign the back of the EC card and memorise your PIN code. You will need the PIN code for cashless payments and to withdraw money from ATMs. Make sure you keep your card and your PIN code separate.
If you want to withdraw cash, you should use the ATMs of the bank where you have your account.Then the withdrawal is free. If you enter the PIN code at the ATM incorrectly 3 times, the EC card will be confiscated by the machine and blocked. In this case, you must ask your bank.
If you lose your EC card or it is stolen, have it blocked immediately.Tell them your sort code and your account number. Then ask your bank for a new EC card.
 116 116 Available around the clock.


If you need assistance with using the EC card correctly, ask your help centre.

A current account is an account for people who want to make payments via the bank. Your money is available in your current account at any time.

With a current account you can:

  • transfer money,
  • receive cashless payments (disbursements from agencies/authorities (social benefits) are often made cashless, in other words, transferred to a personal current account),
  • set up standing orders,
  • use direct debiting,
  • cash a cheque, pay cashless with an EC/Maestro card,
  • withdraw money at the bank counter or an ATM
  • and print your bank statements.

Important:Make sure that you always have money in your bank account.

Tip: Different banks offer current accounts with different conditions. Find out exactly how much a current account costs each month and what services the bank offers.

Opening an account

Decide which bank you want to have an account with. Make an appointment to open the account. You must bring the confirmation from Social Services and your ID or your proof of arrival to the appointment. Please bring one of your identity documents with you: Proof of arrival, residence permit, ID card.If no current address is recorded on the identity document, please provide a registration certificate or similar document.

Important: If you do not speak German or English, please take an interpreter along with you.

Important to note:
  • Once your bank account has been opened, you will receive an EC debit card and a four-digit PIN code.Sign the back of the EC card and memorise your PIN code. You will need the PIN code for cashless payments and to withdraw money from ATMs. Make sure you keep your card and your PIN code separate.
  • If you want to withdraw cash, you should use the ATMs of the bank where you have your account.Then the withdrawal is free. If you enter the PIN code at the ATM incorrectly 3 times, the EC card will be confiscated by the machine and blocked. In this case, you must ask your bank.
  • If you lose your EC card or it is stolen, have it blocked immediately.Tell them your sort code and your account number. Then ask your bank for a new EC card.

     116 116
     Available around the clock.

If you need assistance with using the EC card correctly, ask your help centre.

title: Food
excerpt:
Buying food
In Germany, there are many cheap discount shops and possibly slightly more expensive supermarkets (where mainly branded products are sold). In discount shops and supermarkets you can buy food for everyday needs. Food from specific countries or regions of the world can be found in a well-stocked supermarket or in smaller stores in the district. There are also smaller specialty shops, such as bakeries and butchers, which are spread throughout the city. In drugstores, you find hardly any food, but different toiletries and household products. For example, you can buy nappies and cleaning supplies.
Different halal products and kosher food are available in a well-stocked supermarket. However, there are also food stores that specialise in this. More information:  »»Halal and kosher
In the video from the "Marhaba - arriving in Germany" series, a Lebanese cook talks about the similarities and differences of German and Arabic cuisine: »»Food& Drink – German cuisine is so different.
Nürnberger Tafel e.V.
The Nürnberger Tafel e.V. helps people in Nuremberg, who do not have a lot of money. You can also pick up food and hygiene items at the Nürnberger Tafel e.V. free of charge if you do not earn money yourself or do not earn enough money.
Before you can go spending, you need an identification sheet. This can be issued at the central office. To obtain this, you need a “proof of need”. You will receive this with your temporary resident permit/ID card and the asylum-seekers' notice from Social Services (this is the letter from Social Services, which tells you how much money you get for one month).
Nürnberger Tafel e.V.  Kreutzerstr. 82, 90439 Nuremberg

title: Mobile phones
excerpt:
In Germany, there are generally two different types of mobile phone (“Handy”) contracts: Pre-paid contract and term contract. The prepaid contract does not have a fixed term. The fixed-term contact has a minimum contract period. This contract will be renewed automatically if it is not terminated. If you do not want this contract to be renewed automatically you have to make this known in writing. Make sure to observe the notice period.
Ask your help centre for help, so that you can choose and finalise a good contract.
10 important questions about taking out a contract:

What is the monthly fee? Does the fee increase after a certain period?
Is there a minimum monthly fee?
How long is the contract period (e.g. 12 or 24 months)?
If I don’t terminate the contract, will it be renewed automatically?When do I have to terminate the contract if I do not want it to be renewed?
How much are the fees for setting up, changing or deactivating?
What is the charging period (60/1, 10/10)?
How much does a minute cost (different/own network)?
What are the costs for buying a mobile phone with and without a contract?
How much does Internet access cost?
How much does it cost to call abroad?
content:
In Germany, there are generally two different types of mobile phone (“Handy”) contracts: Pre-paid contract and term contract. The prepaid contract does not have a fixed term. The fixed-term contact has a minimum contract period. This contract will be renewed automatically if it is not terminated. If you do not want this contract to be renewed automatically you have to make this known in writing. Make sure to observe the notice period.

Ask your help centre for help, so that you can choose and finalise a good contract.

10 important questions about taking out a contract:

  1. What is the monthly fee? Does the fee increase after a certain period?
  2. Is there a minimum monthly fee?
  3. How long is the contract period (e.g. 12 or 24 months)?
  4. If I don’t terminate the contract, will it be renewed automatically?When do I have to terminate the contract if I do not want it to be renewed?
  5. How much are the fees for setting up, changing or deactivating?
  6. What is the charging period (60/1, 10/10)?
  7. How much does a minute cost (different/own network)?
  8. What are the costs for buying a mobile phone with and without a contract?
  9. How much does Internet access cost?
  10. How much does it cost to call abroad?

title: Debt counselling
excerpt:
General debt counselling
Is your account overdrawn and the bank won’t pay out any more money? Is the bailiff at the door? Do you have financial problems? The debt counselling office advises and informs you on these matters and many others. The advice is free and confidential.
ISKA debt counselling  Untere Krämersgasse 3, 90403 Nuremberg  +49 (0) 911 24463 0  Monday, Tuesday, Thursday, Friday 09:00 - 15:00 and by appointment.
 The counselling is offered in German. If you need a language specialist, please contact ZAB:»»How do I find an interpreter?
Energy debt
Are you having problems paying bills for household utilities, electricity, heating, gas, hot water or community heating due to ongoing money problems? This means that you have energy debts. These are so-called primary debts. This includes debts that can directly endanger your livelihood and that of your household members. For this purpose, there are special advice centres:
Energy debt counselling www.nuernberg.de/internet/esp/
Obtaining SGB II benefits Your personal contact person or case manager at the job centre
Families with children and young people without a claim to Hartz IV benefitsDistrict social pedagogues at Child Welfare Services at the General Social Service (ASD) +49 (0) 911 231-2686
Individuals and households, all over 21 years of age Social pedagogy at the social pedagogical specialist service (SFD) +49 (0) 911 231-8103
Advice on how to avoid energy debt can be found here:

title: Nuremberg pass
excerpt:
What is the Nuremberg pass?
The Nuremberg Pass is an offer by Social Services of the city of Nuremberg to support people in Nuremberg who do not have much money. Many offers in the fields of education, culture, leisure and sport as well as bus, underground and tram all cost less. In addition, certain pharmacies offer discounts on non-prescription medicines.
Who can apply for the Nuremberg Pass?
Anyone who receives benefits from the job centre, Social Services, the Housing Office (housing allowance) or Child Welfare Services (subsidies on fees for children's day-care facilities, etc.).
Where to apply for it?
To apply for this, you need an ID card or passport and a letter from one of the official authorities (Child Welfare Services, Social Services, Housing Office).
Responsible for the following post codes: 90402, 90411, 90425, 90431, 90443, 90482, 90491, 90403, 90419, 90427, 90439, 90449, 90489, 90408, 90429, 90409
Service centre education and participation – Innenstadt  Frauentorgraben 17, ground floor, 90443 Nuremberg
Responsible for the following post codes: 90441, 90451, 90461, 90471, 90480, 90453, 90469, 90473, 90455, 90475, 90459, 90478
Service centre education and participation – Langwasser  Reinerzer Straße 12, 90473 Nuremberg
 +49 (0) 911 231-4347  Monday - Friday 08:30 - 12:30 www.nuernberg.de/internet/sozialamt/
A multilingual information flyer can be found here:»»Arabic, »»Russian, »»English.
Take a look at»»the film You Are Here! There, all important information about the Nuremberg Pass is summarised.

title: Consumer Advice Centre
excerpt:
What is private liability? How do I cancel my broadcast license fee? When is a product in Germany "Halal"? The Consumer Advice Centre will help you. Consumer Advice Centres are located in every federal state. Here, consumers receive current, reliable information and independent advice. The aim of their work is to provide consumers with information, advice and support for private consumption. They provide an overview in case of confusing offers and transparency in complex market conditions. And they point out health or environmental issues that can influence purchasing decisions. Information is also available in many languages: »»Arabic, »»Farsi, »»Turkish

content:
What is private liability? How do I cancel my broadcast license fee? When is a product in Germany "Halal"? The Consumer Advice Centre will help you. Consumer Advice Centres are located in every federal state. Here, consumers receive current, reliable information and independent advice. The aim of their work is to provide consumers with information, advice and support for private consumption. They provide an overview in case of confusing offers and transparency in complex market conditions. And they point out health or environmental issues that can influence purchasing decisions. Information is also available in many languages: »»Arabic, »»Farsi, »»Turkish

title: Housing
excerpt:
In big cities, it’s often difficult to find a flat. Even in Nuremberg, the search can take several months. It is important to remain patient. Sometimes things don’t work out straight away.
In addition, there are guidelines for benefit recipients that limit the choice of flats. This means that, as a recipient of ALG II or social security benefits, you’ll need to make a special effort to find a suitable flat.
The ‘Housing Guide’ answers questions that are frequently asked when looking for a flat. For each question, you’ll be given the details of a contact person you can get in touch with.
content:
In big cities, it’s often difficult to find a flat. 
Even in Nuremberg, the search can take several months. It is important to remain patient. Sometimes things don’t work out straight away.

In addition, there are guidelines for benefit recipients that limit the choice of flats. This means that, as a recipient of ALG II or social security benefits, you’ll need to make a special effort to find a suitable flat.

The ‘Housing Guide’ answers questions that are frequently asked when looking for a flat. For each question, you’ll be given the details of a contact person you can get in touch with.

title: Looking for Somewhere to Live
excerpt:
Housing Allowance Payments
If you and your household (this means family, partner or children – not roommates/housemates) earn a lower amount of money, you can apply for a subsidised flat or house. You need a Public Housing Certificate to be eligible for this. Under certain conditions, you are also entitled to housing allowance payments. If you get money from the state for yourself and your family, you usually will not have to apply for housing allowance payments.

title: Moving into Your Own Home
excerpt:
As soon as you have a residence permit, you are usually obliged to move out of your community accommodation/decentralised accommodation. You will need to find your own place to live. If you don't have a job and thus aren't earning money, the »»Job Centrewill pay your rent. But it will only do this if you need help paying your rent.
Important: once you have found an apartment, you should not sign the lease immediately. The Job Centre has to check and approve the rent contract before you sign it. The rental contract must include the following points:

"Cold" monthly rent:The rental costs for the living space per month, excluding service and utility costs such as heating

Additional costs: this includes operating costs like stairwell cleaning, waste collection charges, caretaker costs, cable connection etc.


Heating costs: Costs for heating and hot water (note: you always have to pay electricity and heating costs yourself!)


Size of apartment, number of rooms, address, landlord


 The "warm" rent is included everything mentioned above. It is therefore significantly higher than the monthly rent.
Deposit, initial living supplies, one-off payments for fixtures and fittings, and cancellation fee
The deposit is a security payment you make to the landlord. The amount of the deposit is a maximum of 3 months' rent. When you move out of the property, you get the money back.
Important: You must not leave any damage if you move out of the property.
You are not allowed to take the furniture from your asylum accommodation. That's why you need new furniture. You can submit a request for initial supplies to the Job Centre. You will then receive a flat-rate amount from the Job Centre. This amount can vary according to the number of people and the size of the property. Find cheap and used furniture or other furnishings here:  »»Furniture, Crockery etc.. If the person who was previously living in the home leaves fixtures and fittings there, you can buy those things. In German, this is called "Ablöse".
Attention: It may take a few weeks for the money to be paid by the Job Centre!
Please remember to re-register, in other words you need to re-register your new address (in person) at the »»Residents' Registration Office.
content:
As soon as you have a residence permit, you are usually obliged to move out of your community accommodation/decentralised accommodation. You will need to find your own place to live. If you don't have a job and thus aren't earning money, the »»Job Centrewill pay your rent. But it will only do this if you need help paying your rent.

Important: once you have found an apartment, you should not sign the lease immediately. The Job Centre has to check and approve the rent contract before you sign it. The rental contract must include the following points:

  • "Cold" monthly rent:The rental costs for the living space per month, excluding service and utility costs such as heating
  • Additional costs: this includes operating costs like stairwell cleaning, waste collection charges, caretaker costs, cable connection etc.
  • Heating costs: Costs for heating and hot water (note: you always have to pay electricity and heating costs yourself!)
  • Size of apartment, number of rooms, address, landlord

The "warm" rent is included everything mentioned above. It is therefore significantly higher than the monthly rent.

Deposit, initial living supplies, one-off payments for fixtures and fittings, and cancellation fee

The deposit is a security payment you make to the landlord. The amount of the deposit is a maximum of 3 months' rent. When you move out of the property, you get the money back.

Important: You must not leave any damage if you move out of the property.

You are not allowed to take the furniture from your asylum accommodation. That's why you need new furniture. You can submit a request for initial supplies to the Job Centre. You will then receive a flat-rate amount from the Job Centre. This amount can vary according to the number of people and the size of the property. Find cheap and used furniture or other furnishings here:  »»Furniture, Crockery etc.. If the person who was previously living in the home leaves fixtures and fittings there, you can buy those things. In German, this is called "Ablöse".

Attention: It may take a few weeks for the money to be paid by the Job Centre!

Please remember to re-register, in other words you need to re-register your new address (in person) at the »»Residents' Registration Office.

title: House rules
excerpt:
There are certain regulations for living together that are defined as house rules. These apply to all persons who live in shared accommodation or an apartment. The most important content is:
Noise: Everyone in the house is responsible for keeping the noise level to a minimum. Special care must be taken between 13:00 and 15:00 and between 22:00 and 06:00, which are rest periods. On Sundays and public holidays the whole day is considered as a rest period.
Waste disposal:Waste must be disposed of in the designated bin. There are different bins for plastic, paper and cardboard, fruit and vegetables (organic waste) and other waste. Please respect the correct rubbish separation, because the majority of rubbish is recycled and it is an important part of environmental protection.
You can see an overview of the waste that belongs in each bin here:»»Waste separation in pictures.
Ventilation: You should regularly open the windows to let fresh air in. This prevents mould from forming.
content:
There are certain regulations for living together that are defined as house rules. These apply to all persons who live in shared accommodation or an apartment. The most important content is:

Waste disposal:Waste must be disposed of in the designated bin. There are different bins for plastic, paper and cardboard, fruit and vegetables (organic waste) and other waste. Please respect the correct rubbish separation, because the majority of rubbish is recycled and it is an important part of environmental protection.

You can see an overview of the waste that belongs in each bin here:»»Waste separation in pictures.

Ventilation: You should regularly open the windows to let fresh air in. This prevents mould from forming.

title: Heating tips
excerpt:
Save heating
 Set the heating less warm and ventilate properly. 
Heat less warmly:
 The rooms in your home do not all have to be equally warm. This will save you money.
 
What should you do during the day?

In the living room, 20 degrees is good.
In the bedroom, 16 degrees is good.
In the bathroom 23 degrees is good.

 Are you in some of the rooms only rarely? Then 16 degrees is also good here.
What should you do at night?

Set the heating to a lower temperature. 
Then 16 degrees is good in all rooms. 

What else can you do?

Do not put things in front of the heating.
Do not hang curtains in front of the heating.
In winter it gets dark early outside. Then lower the blinds. That way the warm air stays in the room better.


Picture source: Thermostats - Numbers and their meaning (heizsparer.de) 
 
Ventilate properly
 Do not keep tilting the window. Otherwise the walls of the room will get cold and you will have to heat more afterwards. 
How do I ventilate properly?

Open the window all the way.
Leave the window open for 5 to 10 minutes. 
Repeat this 2 or 3 times a day. This will bring in fresh air. 

 Always turn off the heating when airing. This will save you energy and money. 
What is important in winter?

In winter, you do not have to ventilate for as long. 
Only open the window for 2 to 3 minutes.
content:
Save heating

Set the heating less warm and ventilate properly.

Heat less warmly:

The rooms in your home do not all have to be equally warm. This will save you money.

What should you do during the day?

  • In the living room, 20 degrees is good.
  • In the bedroom, 16 degrees is good.
  • In the bathroom 23 degrees is good.

Are you in some of the rooms only rarely? Then 16 degrees is also good here.

What should you do at night?

  • Set the heating to a lower temperature.
  • Then 16 degrees is good in all rooms.

What else can you do?

  • Do not put things in front of the heating.
  • Do not hang curtains in front of the heating.
  • In winter it gets dark early outside. Then lower the blinds. That way the warm air stays in the room better.

Picture source: Thermostats - Numbers and their meaning (heizsparer.de)

Ventilate properly

Do not keep tilting the window. Otherwise the walls of the room will get cold and you will have to heat more afterwards.

How do I ventilate properly?

  • Open the window all the way.
  • Leave the window open for 5 to 10 minutes.
  • Repeat this 2 or 3 times a day. This will bring in fresh air.

Always turn off the heating when airing. This will save you energy and money.

What is important in winter?

  • In winter, you do not have to ventilate for as long.
  • Only open the window for 2 to 3 minutes.

title: Power tips
excerpt:
Save electricity
 You can save electricity on many everyday tasks such as cooking or washing clothes.
Cooking:

Use a lid when cooking. 
If you cook with a small pot or pan, use a small cooker top.
If you cook with a large pot or pan, use a large hotplate.
Turn off the cooker a little earlier. The heat will stay a little longer and you will continue cooking without electricity.

Do you have an old cooker?
 Look at the electricity consumption of your cooker. It might be a good idea to buy a new cooker. A new cooker uses much less electricity.
Do you need hot water?
 Then make the water hot in the kettle. The kettle uses less electricity than the cooker. Only make as much water hot in the kettle as you need. 
Use the oven properly:

Do not preheat the oven. 
Do not open the oven door too often.
Turn off the oven 10 minutes earlier.

Electrical appliances:
 Turn off the TV, computer, radio or electric toothbrush completely. To do this, turn off the switch or pull the plug completely out of the socket. 
Lamps and lights:

Old light bulbs consume a lot of electricity.
Energy-saving bulbs or LED bulbs consume much less electricity and do not break down as quickly. 
If you don't need light at the moment, turn the lamps off.

Save electricity in the refrigerator:

Set the fridge to 7 degrees. 7 degrees is enough for food.
Don't open the fridge so often. Otherwise the inside of the fridge will get too warm and consume more electricity. 
Do not put warm food in the refrigerator. Otherwise the inside of the fridge will get too warm and use more electricity. 
Do not put the refrigerator next to a dishwasher, next to a heater or in a sunny place. Otherwise, the refrigerator will have to cool much more and will consume more electricity. 
The refrigerator has a grille at the back for ventilation. Clean the grille regularly or the ventilation will not work as well and the refrigerator will consume more electricity. 

Saving electricity with the freezer:

Set the freezer to minus 18 degrees. 
Do not open the freezer so often. Otherwise the freezer will get too warm inside and use more electricity. 
Do not put warm food in the freezer. Otherwise the inside of the freezer will get too warm and use more electricity. 
Remove thick layers of ice. Otherwise the freezer will use more electricity.

 Look for the EU energy label (EU label). There are different letters on the label. These letters stand for the power consumption. A means the appliance consumes very little electricity. The letter G means the appliance consumes a lot of electricity.
content:
Save electricity

You can save electricity on many everyday tasks such as cooking or washing clothes.

Cooking:

  • Use a lid when cooking.
  • If you cook with a small pot or pan, use a small cooker top.
  • If you cook with a large pot or pan, use a large hotplate.
  • Turn off the cooker a little earlier. The heat will stay a little longer and you will continue cooking without electricity.

Do you have an old cooker?

Look at the electricity consumption of your cooker. It might be a good idea to buy a new cooker. A new cooker uses much less electricity.

Do you need hot water?

Then make the water hot in the kettle. The kettle uses less electricity than the cooker. Only make as much water hot in the kettle as you need.

Use the oven properly:

  • Do not preheat the oven.
  • Do not open the oven door too often.
  • Turn off the oven 10 minutes earlier.

Electrical appliances:

Turn off the TV, computer, radio or electric toothbrush completely. To do this, turn off the switch or pull the plug completely out of the socket.

Lamps and lights:

  • Old light bulbs consume a lot of electricity.
  • Energy-saving bulbs or LED bulbs consume much less electricity and do not break down as quickly.
  • If you don't need light at the moment, turn the lamps off.

Save electricity in the refrigerator:

  • Set the fridge to 7 degrees. 7 degrees is enough for food.
  • Don't open the fridge so often. Otherwise the inside of the fridge will get too warm and consume more electricity.
  • Do not put warm food in the refrigerator. Otherwise the inside of the fridge will get too warm and use more electricity.
  • Do not put the refrigerator next to a dishwasher, next to a heater or in a sunny place. Otherwise, the refrigerator will have to cool much more and will consume more electricity.
  • The refrigerator has a grille at the back for ventilation. Clean the grille regularly or the ventilation will not work as well and the refrigerator will consume more electricity.

Saving electricity with the freezer:

  • Set the freezer to minus 18 degrees.
  • Do not open the freezer so often. Otherwise the freezer will get too warm inside and use more electricity.
  • Do not put warm food in the freezer. Otherwise the inside of the freezer will get too warm and use more electricity.
  • Remove thick layers of ice. Otherwise the freezer will use more electricity.

Look for the EU energy label (EU label). There are different letters on the label. These letters stand for the power consumption. A means the appliance consumes very little electricity. The letter G means the appliance consumes a lot of electricity.

title: With or without a job - support from the Job Centre for heating costs
excerpt:
Did you know? Despite having a job, many people can get money from the Job Centre for their heating costs. Caritas Germany shows how.
It is not true that only people who already receive unemployment benefit II are supported by the Job Centre. There are many people with a job who are also entitled to a heating allowance from the Job Centre. This is because the entitlement to financial support from the Job Centre always refers to the month in which a bill is due. If you or a friend will soon receive a high utility bill, you may be entitled to support in that month.
It sounds complicated and it is a bit. Therefore, we will show how the whole thing works with an example.
Alex lives alone and earns 2,950 euros gross/month. After deducting taxes, social security contributions, health insurance and church tax, he still has 1,970 euros left over. Of this, 900 euros still have to be paid in rent (warm). In March 2023, there will be an additional heating cost payment of 800 euros.
So much for the exemplary facts, now the calculation. First, the need under social law must be calculated. This is made up of the current standard need for Hartz4, the rent and the heating cost arrears. So: 449 euros plus 900 euros plus 800 euros. The socio-legal need is therefore 2,149 euros.
Since Alex earns money, the net salary must be deducted from this. This is used to see whether the socio-legal need above 2,149 euros can be covered by the monthly salary itself. However, the net salary above 1,970 euros is not taken for this, but only 1,670 euros. This is because in the so-called income adjustment, a basic allowance of 100 euros and an earned income allowance of 200 euros are deducted.
Now for the exciting part of the calculation. So we have to deduct the adjusted net income of 1,670 euros from the social need of 2,149 euros. So: 2,149 euros minus 1,670 euros. The result: 479 euros. In this case, Alex would be entitled to 479 euros from the Job Centre in March.
For this to happen, however, it is absolutely necessary for Alex to apply for support from the Job Centre in the month in which she received the utility bill, i.e. in March in our example. In April, the bill would look different again. Because then the 800 euros for the heating cost arrears would be omitted from the socio-legal need and would amount to 1,349 euros. Alex can cover these needs himself with his monthly salary of 1,670 euros after deducting the allowances.
Anyone who is not sure whether they could be entitled can get information from the many advice centres in Nuremberg and throughout Germany. 
Source: Facebook Caritas Germany
content:
Did you know? Despite having a job, many people can get money from the Job Centre for their heating costs. Caritas Germany shows how.

It is not true that only people who already receive unemployment benefit II are supported by the Job Centre. There are many people with a job who are also entitled to a heating allowance from the Job Centre. This is because the entitlement to financial support from the Job Centre always refers to the month in which a bill is due. If you or a friend will soon receive a high utility bill, you may be entitled to support in that month.

It sounds complicated and it is a bit. Therefore, we will show how the whole thing works with an example.

Alex lives alone and earns 2,950 euros gross/month. After deducting taxes, social security contributions, health insurance and church tax, he still has 1,970 euros left over. Of this, 900 euros still have to be paid in rent (warm). In March 2023, there will be an additional heating cost payment of 800 euros.

So much for the exemplary facts, now the calculation. First, the need under social law must be calculated. This is made up of the current standard need for Hartz4, the rent and the heating cost arrears. So: 449 euros plus 900 euros plus 800 euros. The socio-legal need is therefore 2,149 euros.

Since Alex earns money, the net salary must be deducted from this. This is used to see whether the socio-legal need above 2,149 euros can be covered by the monthly salary itself. However, the net salary above 1,970 euros is not taken for this, but only 1,670 euros. This is because in the so-called income adjustment, a basic allowance of 100 euros and an earned income allowance of 200 euros are deducted.

Now for the exciting part of the calculation. So we have to deduct the adjusted net income of 1,670 euros from the social need of 2,149 euros. So: 2,149 euros minus 1,670 euros. The result: 479 euros. In this case, Alex would be entitled to 479 euros from the Job Centre in March.

For this to happen, however, it is absolutely necessary for Alex to apply for support from the Job Centre in the month in which she received the utility bill, i.e. in March in our example. In April, the bill would look different again. Because then the 800 euros for the heating cost arrears would be omitted from the socio-legal need and would amount to 1,349 euros. Alex can cover these needs himself with his monthly salary of 1,670 euros after deducting the allowances.

Anyone who is not sure whether they could be entitled can get information from the many advice centres in Nuremberg and throughout Germany.

Source: Facebook Caritas Germany

title: Sport and exercise
excerpt: Fun in movement is one option. But sport offers you another chance: Sport is a good and proven way to settle in and integrate into a new environment. The rules of sport do not know any cultural or national boundaries and are usually understood without a common language. Sport offers a good and informal possibility to make contact with locals through personal contacts.
content: Fun in movement is one option. But sport offers you another chance: Sport is a good and proven way to settle in and integrate into a new environment. The rules of sport do not know any cultural or national boundaries and are usually understood without a common language. Sport offers a good and informal possibility to make contact with locals through personal contacts.

title: Sport service
excerpt:
Would you like to learn to swim? Or play football? Or dance? There are four indoor pools in Nuremberg. They are heated and open all year round. Outdoor swimming pools are only open in summer and are open air. In all of the indoor and outdoor swimming pools, you have to observe the bathing rules.
 Prices may vary. School children, students, holders of »»Nuremberg passes and severely handicapped persons pay a discounted price.

title: Parks and Green Spaces
excerpt:
Parks and green spaces are important for a city. They provide space for relaxation, exercise and leisure activities. Green spaces make the city a better place to live and offer a space for nature right in the heart of the city.
There are many parks and green spaces in Nuremberg. They are very large. You can go for a walk or a cycle there. There are also football pitches and large meadows. Well-known parks include Marienbergpark, Wöhrder Wiese and Stadtpark.
You can find more information about the parks and green spaces in Nuremberg here:
 www.nuernberg.de/internet/stadtportal/parks.html
North Nuremberg

South Nuremberg
 Dutzendteich Public Park
 Luitpoldhain (Ludwigsfeld)

 Hallerwiese (St. Johannis)

Further inner-city belt

West
 Westpark (Sündersbühl, Gaismannshof)
Need more information? Here’s an overview of all public barbecue areas in Nuremberg:»»Barbecue areas

title: BBQ areas
excerpt:
In summer, many people enjoy barbecuing. The smell of meat and fresh vegetables is part of the experience. Barbecuing is only permitted at public barbecue areas. These areas are specifically designated for this purpose. There are five public barbecue areas in Nuremberg.
Signposted barbecue areas in Nuremberg
Dutzendteich  A barbecue area is situated between the Silbersee and the Kleiner Dutzendteich. It is in Nuremberg.
Pegnitztal West West of the Theodor-Heuss-Brücke and west of the Lederersteg
Marienbergpark Two possible barbecue areas: in the central section on Kilianstraße and in the northern section on Marienbergstraße.
Rednitztal East On Ellwanger Straße.
Stadionbad
 Please take your rubbish away with you. You can dispose of any charcoal remains in the designated charcoal bins.
Further information on Nuremberg’s barbecue areas can be found on the City of Nuremberg’s website:
 www.nuernberg.de/internet/stadtportal/grillplaetze

title: Culture ticket
excerpt:
If you have a »»Nuremberg pass , you can get free entry to many cultural events. There is a maximum of 2 tickets per Nuremberg Pass for each event. This offer is called KulturTicket (culture ticket). It is abbreviated to KTN (Kultur-Ticket-Nürnberg) (Culture Ticket Nuremberg). You have to register if you want to take advantage of this great offer.
A personal consultation is available every Thursday from 11:00 to 14:00 in the foyer of the Kunsthaus:
KunstKulturQuartier  Königstraße 93, 90443 Nuremberg, First Floor
More information at:
 www.buergerstiftung-nuernberg.de/kulturticket/
How do I register?

Complete the registration form: »»KTN registration. 
Sign and complete the registration form and return it together with a copy of your own Nuremberg pass to:   Bürgerstiftung Nürnberg , Nordring 98, 90409 Nuremberg or by e-mail: info@kulturticket-nuernberg.de or in person: KulturInformation, Königstraße 93, 90443 Nuremberg
Following successful registration, you will be called if tickets are left, if you are still interested and want to attend, you can confirm this.
Tickets can then be collected at the box office before the start of the event.
content:
If you have a »»Nuremberg pass , you can get free entry to many cultural events. There is a maximum of 2 tickets per Nuremberg Pass for each event. This offer is called KulturTicket (culture ticket). It is abbreviated to KTN (Kultur-Ticket-Nürnberg) (Culture Ticket Nuremberg). You have to register if you want to take advantage of this great offer.

A personal consultation is available every Thursday from 11:00 to 14:00 in the foyer of the Kunsthaus:

KunstKulturQuartier
 Königstraße 93, 90443 Nuremberg, First Floor

More information at:

www.buergerstiftung-nuernberg.de/kulturticket/

How do I register?

  1. Complete the registration form: »»KTN registration. 
  2. Sign and complete the registration form and return it together with a copy of your own Nuremberg pass to:
     Bürgerstiftung Nürnberg , Nordring 98, 90409 Nuremberg or by e-mail:
     info@kulturticket-nuernberg.de or in person:
    KulturInformation, Königstraße 93, 90443 Nuremberg
  3. Following successful registration, you will be called if tickets are left, if you are still interested and want to attend, you can confirm this.
  4. Tickets can then be collected at the box office before the start of the event.

title: City Library
excerpt:
The City Library and District Libraries are places where you can borrow various media. There's a lot on offer: you can borrow books for children, teenagers and adults, learning aids, resources for learning German, cassettes, CDs, CD-ROMs and DVDs along with books in different languages.The Central City Library also has many cosy seating areas for learning (»»Learning Spots) and for reading.
To borrow something, you need a library card. You have to bring your temporary residence permit or your certificate granting temporary postponement of deportation (Duldung). You also have to pay an annual fee to borrow things.Adults aged 18 and over pay €20 for one year. Students, school pupils and Nuremberg Pass holders pay €10 for one year. For children and teenagers under the age of 18, borrowing things is free of charge.
 You can take books home with you for 28 days and DVDs for 14 days. After four weeks, you have to return everything. Important: If you do not bring the items back in time, you will have to pay high fees!
Further information  on how to use the library and how to register can be found here as a PDF »»German, »»English , »»French, »»Persian, »»Arabic, »»Russian and »»Turkish.
In addition to the Central City Library, there are six district libraries in Nuremberg. As of 2018, all libraries have free Wi-Fi. Learning in the library and using the Wi-Fi are free of charge.
Take a look at You Are Here's film about the City Library: »»You Are Here - City Library.

Südstadt
 Pillenreuther Str. 147, 90459 Nürnberg Tuesday - Friday 14:00 - 18:00

title: Culture pilots
excerpt:
Would you like to get to know Nuremberg’s cultural scene? Would you like to go to a concert, but you don’t want to go alone? - Then go with the culture pilots! These are committed people from Nuremberg who will pick you up at the community accommodation, go with you to a concert or to another cultural event and then accompany you back again.
Do you want to know more? Then take a look at the film of You Are Here about the culture pilots:»»Film You Are Here - culture pilots.
content:
Would you like to get to know Nuremberg’s cultural scene? Would you like to go to a concert, but you don’t want to go alone? - Then go with the culture pilots! These are committed people from Nuremberg who will pick you up at the community accommodation, go with you to a concert or to another cultural event and then accompany you back again.

Do you want to know more? Then take a look at the film of You Are Here about the culture pilots:»»Film You Are Here - culture pilots.

title: Museums
excerpt:
Museums in the City of Nuremberg
If you have a »»Nuremberg Pass, you only have to pay an entrance fee of €1.50 to visit several municipal museums on one day. On International Museum Day, you can get a day ticket for all museums in Nuremberg for €3.00. There are also many things to do for children.
 www.museen.nuernberg.de
The museums of the City of Nuremberg include:

Albrecht-Dürer-Haus
Albrecht-Dürer-Straße 39, 90403 Nürnberg

Tucherschloss and Hirsvogelsaal Museum
 Hirschelgasse 9-11, 90403 Nürnberg<

Fembo-Haus City Museum
  Burgstraße 15, 90403 Nürnberg

Museum of Industrial Culture (Museum Industriekultur)
 Äußere Sulzbacher Straße 62, 90491 Nürnberg

School Museum (Schulmuseum)
 Äußere Sulzbacher Straße 62, 90491 Nürnberg

Toy Museum (Spielzeugmuseum)
 Karlstraße 13-15, 90403 Nürnberg

Documentation Centre at the Former Nazi Party Rally Grounds (Dokumentationszentrum Reichsparteitagsgelände)
 Bayernstraße 110, 90478 Nürnberg

Nuremberg Trials Memorial (Memorium Nürnberger Prozesse)
  Bärenschanzstraße 72, 90429 Nürnberg

Other Museums in Nuremberg
In addition to the municipal museums in Nuremberg, there are other museums in the city:

Germanic National Museum (GNM)
The Germanic National Museum is the largest museum of German art and culture and can be visited free of charge every Wednesday from 17:30 – 20:30.
 Kartäusergasse 1, 90402 Nürnberg  www.gnm.de

Neues Museum Nuremberg (NMN)
The Neues Museum in Nuremberg is a state museum for art and design. Every Sunday the entrance fee is only €1. Entrance is free for children and teenagers up to 18 years old!
 Luitpoldstraße 5, 90402 Nürnberg  www.nmn.de

DB Nuremberg
DB Museum is the oldest railway museum in the world. Entrance is free for children aged under 6!
  Lessingstraße 6, 90443 Nürnberg  www.dbmuseum.de

Children's and Youth Museum Nuremberg
Here, children can do more than just look – they can also join in and have a hands on experience. Children under 3 years old can enter for free.
  Im Kachelbau, Michael-Ende-Straße 17, 90439 Nürnberg www.kindermuseum-nuernberg.de

Nuremberg Museum of Communication
The Nuremberg Museum of Communication shows the history of post and news transmission in Bavaria, from the Middle Ages to the present day. Entrance is free for children aged under 6!
 Lessingstraße 6, 90443 Nürnberg (DB Museum) www.mfk-nuernberg.de

For more information and tips in several languages, please visit the»»You Are Here website:
 www.refugees-nuernberg.de

title: You Are Here
excerpt:
You Are Here is a group of volunteers, local cultural workers and asylum seekers. They have created a multilingual online culture guide. It is intended to provide an overview of the cultural life of Nuremberg to everyone who is new to the city, thus helping them in their social integration.
The guide is currently available in five languages: German, English, Arabic, Persian and Kurdish. It will include clubs, museums, cultural associations and cultural locations. There are also event tips as well as video reports on topics like city life to German customs (such as the whimsical carnival crash course "How to Fasching").
 www.refugees-nuernberg.de
content:
You Are Here is a group of volunteers, local cultural workers and asylum seekers. They have created a multilingual online culture guide. It is intended to provide an overview of the cultural life of Nuremberg to everyone who is new to the city, thus helping them in their social integration.

The guide is currently available in five languages: German, English, Arabic, Persian and Kurdish. It will include clubs, museums, cultural associations and cultural locations. There are also event tips as well as video reports on topics like city life to German customs (such as the whimsical carnival crash course "How to Fasching").

 www.refugees-nuernberg.de

title: Theatre, opera and event venues
excerpt:
State Theatre Nuremberg Opera house
If you have a »»Nuremberg pass, you only pay half the entry price. You can also get tickets for free with the »»culture tickets.
 Richard-Wagner-Platz www.staatstheater-nuernberg.de


Nuremberg Symphony Orchestra 
The symphony orchestra are partners of the »»culture tickets and you only pay half of the entry price with the »»Nuremberg pass.
www.nuernbergersymphoniker.de


Gostner Hoftheater
The Gostner Hoftheater is Nuremberg's oldest private theatre. With the »»Nuremberg pass you pay less for admission and you can get tickets with »»culture tickets.
Austrage 70, 90429 Nuremberg  www.gostner.de


Theater Salz und Pfeffer
 The puppet and figure theatre offers a colourful programme for adults and children. With the »»Nuremberg pass you pay less for the entry price.
 Frauentorgraben 73, 90443 Nuremberg www.salzundpfeffer-theater.de


Theater Pfütze
Exciting theatre plays for both young and old are on offer in the Theater Pfütze programme. With the »»Nuremberg pass you pay less for the entry price.
Äußerer Laufer Platz 22, 90403 Nuremberg  www.theater-pfuetze.de


Theater Mummpitz
The Theater Mummpitz is a children's theatre, but it also impresses adults. With the »»Nuremberg pass you only pay half the entry price.
 Michael-Ende-Straße 17, 90439 Nuremberg  www.theater-mummpitz.de


Theater Rootslöffel
The Theater Rootslöffel mainly offers its own productions, which are suitable for four years old and upwards.
 Troststraße 6, 90429 Nuremberg  www.rootsloeffel.de


Burgtheater
The Nuremberg Burgtheater exclusively shows cabaret, satire, and comedy. With the »»Nuremberg pass you pay a reduced entry price.
 Füll 13, 90403 Nuremberg  burgtheater.de


Meistersingerhalle
Here you can find concerts, balls, theatre shows and much more.
 Münchener Straße 21, 90478 Nuremberg  www.nuernberg.de


Z-Bau
Nowadays the former barracks is a cultural location with many offerings. From the beer garden and concerts to art exhibitions and activities to take part in.
 Frankenstraße 200, 90461 Nuremberg  z-bau.com

title: Festivals
excerpt:
Every year, many free festivals take place in Nuremberg. Everyone can attend!

Africa Festival
Once a year, there's an African theme under the Theodor-Heuss-Brücke in the heart of Nuremberg. Relax in the African market, take a stroll surrounded by African products, or indulge yourself at one of the many food stalls. Taking place once a year to the sound of a range of bands, the festival is free to attend.
 www.afrikafestivalnuernberg.de

Old Town Festival
Get together in the cosy settings of beer huts, drink a little with friends and acquaintances and hear a little of music too – right in the middle of the city. During 10 days of September, theInsel-Schütt turns into a large bar. The Old Town Festival welcomes everyone.
 www.altstadtfest-nbg.de

Bardentreffen
The Bardentreffen takes place in the city centre in late July/early August. Over the weekend, about 100 artists and bands perform across nine stages. For children, there's an extra stage on theInsel Schütt. Entrance to all the concerts is free.
 www.bardentreffen.nuernberg.de

Brückenfestival
Local and international bands perform under theTheodor-Heuss-Brücke on Brückenfestival weekend. In addition to the music, there are a variety of hands-on activities and a play area for children. At the Pegnitzauen, everyone is invited to join in the celebration.
 www.bruecken-festival.de

Die Blaue Nacht
The Blaue Nacht is all about art, and there's always a guiding theme too. At various points in the city, performances and installations are held from 19:00 to midnight in various parts of the city. Blaue Nacht always takes place in May. Some of the performances are free – these include the large installation at the castle and the performances at the church.
 www.blauenacht.nuernberg.de

Classic Open Air
for the Classic Open Air, theLuitpoldhain at Dutzendteich is transformed into a concert hall twice a year. You can take a picnic along with you and listen without distraction to classical music performed by various Nuremberg orchestras – and at the end, you can marvel at the huge firework display. This event is free.

 
 
 
 
 
        www.klassikopenair.nuernberg.de
 
 

Südstadtfest
Once a year, Südstadt hosts a celebration with the motto Eat, Party, Encounter and Get Informed. For one long weekend, Annapark  is transformed into a multicultural meeting place with music, dance and much much more. Entry is also free.
 www.suedstadtfest.de

NN-Rockbühne
The battle for a place at Rock im Park involves 16 bands and lasts a whole year. There are bands of all genres, so you can always look forward to the amazing mix of sounds at the pre-qualification rounds that take place at Quibbel. The concerts are free to attend.
 www.quibble.de

title: Film Festivals
excerpt:
Nuremberg has a very large cinema. TheCinecitta is one of the largest cinemas in Europe.

Turkey-Germany Film Festival
At this festival, Turkish and German films are shown in their original languages. Directors or actors are often in attendance to talk about their films.
 www.fftd.net


Nuremberg International Human Rights Film Festival
This film festival takes place every two years. It screens films with political and social themes.
 www.nihrff.de


Summer Night Film Festival
In August, you can watch films under the open sky in many places.
 www.sommernachtfilmfestival.de


Mediterranean Film Days
Many films are also shown in their original language at this festival.
 www.mittelmeerfilmtage.de


Spanish Film Festival
Films from Spain, shown in Spanish, are screened here in the open air.
 www.facebook.com/SpanischeFilmtageNuernberg


Greek Film Festival
It’s not just films that are shown here; guests are also invited to take part in discussions.
 www.griechische-filmtage-nbg.de


Polish Film Weeks
During the Polish Film Weeks, films from our neighbouring country, Poland, are screened. Concerts are also held.
 www.polnische-filmwoche.de


Middle Franconian Youth Film Festival
At this festival, young filmmakers and teenagers screen video clips and short films they have produced themselves. Admission is free.
 www.jugendfilmfestival.de

Further information and tips in several languages can be found on the »»You Are Here website :
 www.refugees-nuernberg.de
